title: PAC Prediction Sets for Meta-Learning

reviews.summary:
This paper builds on PAC prediction sets by extending it to few-shot meta-learning settings, where there is assumed access to a i.i.d. sequence of tasks. In few-shot meta-learning, the goal is to adapt to a new task with only a few training examples. In order to hope to do this successfully, the learner is also exposed to a collection of similar tasks (i.e., i.i.d. from some distribution over tasks) before being presented with the test task. From a confidence point of view, providing theoretically valid, non-vacuous confidence sets for few-shot learning is difficult. The work of [1] first proposed a method of also using the task distribution to help derive meaningful conformal prediction sets, that have marginal coverage guarantees over the draw of calibration tasks and test task. This paper adopts this setting, but casts it under the framework of PAC prediction sets, which offer high probability guarantees about the expected mis-coverage over new tasks, conditional on observed calibration tasks. On a high-level, PAC sets is to CP sets as Meta-PAC sets are to Meta-CP sets, and offer a different set of (desireable) performance guarantees. The paper empirically validates the effectiveness (and theoretical properties) of their algorithm on simulated few-shot meta-learning datasets (i.e., K-shot N-way image classification and relation classification tasks), as well as a realistic heart attack prediction task (where the "tasks" are stratified by year and state).

[1] Few-shot conformal prediction with auxiliary tasks. https://arxiv.org/abs/2102.08898.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
The paper is well-written (for the most part, see below) and the experiments are interesting. As the authors do well in pointing out, their PAC calibration framework offers several desireable guarantees that are not covered by the Meta-CP procedure. For example, Meta-PAC sets will be valid on average across all new tasks, rather than just on the (n + 1)th, marginalized over the draw of tasks {1, ..., n + 1}. Like standard PAC sets, this is nice, as it is often more practical to assume that the same fixed calibration set is reused for predictions across many future tasks. Therefore, even though prediction sets have been considered in a meta-learning setting before, this seems like a meaningful contribution.

Apart from a few specific comments/concerns, my main remark is on the readability of the presentation. Granted, I think the paper does an ok job considering the number of factors to consider simultaneously, but the notation is quite burdensome. It may be much easier to follow if a high-level, step-by-step description of what the authors want to accomplish and how they do it, is provided at the start of section 4---instead of jumping right into notation. For example, at a high-level, Meta-PAC sets are trying to find a single, shared threshold to use that 
1. On average over the random draw of calibration tasks that are used to choose this threshold...
2. and on average over the random choice of task that is used to evaluate this threshold...
3. and on average over predictions the model makes using this threshold on the new task...
4. The prediction set contains the correct label.

(Where we can replace "average" with high probability $1-\delta$, $1-\alpha$, $1-\epsilon$.)

I think this message is hinted at in various places in the manuscript, but it would be useful to say it plainly up front (obviously, not exactly in those words, but in simple, intuitive terms).

On to a few high-lighted strengths...

- The theoretical setting is appealing, and a reasonable and useful extension to Meta-CP (as stated above). 
- The calibration strategy seems correct and nice. At first I was concerned that computing PAC-thresholds for each of the calibration tasks was missing a factor of 1/N so that they are valid simultaneously, before applying the PAC method again on top of these values---as, with high probability, many $\hat{\tau}_{\epsilon, \alpha/2}(S_i, A_i)$ will _not_ be in $T_\epsilon(\theta_i, A_i)$ for large N. So we'd be calibrating the final threshold based on some "imposter" thresholds from a subset of tasks {1, ..., N}. However, my understanding is that since we only care about the output of a PAC method being i.i.d. (this is the r.v. O = PS-Binom(...) where O is valid w.p. 1 - alpha), not the output being valid itself, we do not actually need the PAC property to hold simulataneously over calibration tasks. Hence, the authors are able to avoid the 1/N factor in favor of two 1/2 factors (which is way more efficient). 
- Empirical results seem strong.

And a few weaknesses...

- It's kind of bothersome that a single threshold is used for _all_ downstream tasks; i.e., $\tau$ never gets updated, regardless of the size of the adaptation set A. In contrast, the Meta-CP method attempts to also estimate a data-dependent threshold (i.e., the adjusted quantile prediction). Therefore, when there is enough few-shot data, or significant variability across tasks, this could be better (in terms of being test-task conditionally). Here, the threshold never changes, regardless of whether the new task seems incredibly hard or incredibly easy.
- How tight are Clopper-Pearson based bounds when using small calibration task sizes? Assuming access to several hundred i.i.d. calibration tasks seems rather unrealistic, apart from very specific scenarios. The theory is technically solid, but a relatively straightforward combination of previous techniques (Meta-CP and PAC calibration). Thus I anticipate it to have only a relatively modest impact.
reviews.questions:
- In the experiments it seems that you calibrate using tasks sampled from N choose k base calbration classes, and test on N choose k base (disjoint) test classes. Samples from the second are not i.i.d. w.r.t the second?
 - Lines 171-174 I can't understand. From what I can see, you simply want to estimate a value t s.t. $P(\hat{\tau}_{\alpha/2, \delta}(S) \leq t) \geq 1 - \delta$. I'm not sure what $g$ is, and adding the dummy label also seems confusing.
reviews.limitations: The authors adequately addressed the limitations and potential negative societal impact of their work. It might be worth to expand on the last sentence of the conclusion: namely that people should understand that this algorithm is not a cure-all for few-shot confidence estimation, but one that relies on several strong assumptions, and only holds on average across new tasks. That said, it is better than nothing!
reviews.rating: 6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.

The authors adequately addressed the limitations and potential negative societal impact of their work. That said, it is better than nothing!

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
# Hybrid Peer Review: Meta-PS - PAC Prediction Sets for Meta-Learning

## Summary

This paper extends PAC (Probably Approximately Correct) prediction sets to the meta-learning setting, where a learner must adapt to new tasks using only a few training examples while having access to a distribution over related calibration tasks. The authors propose Meta-PS, which provides a three-parameter meta-PAC (mPAC) guarantee (ε, α, δ) that controls error over test examples, test task adaptation sets, and calibration task distributions simultaneously. The key contribution is offering high-probability guarantees about expected mis-coverage over new tasks, conditional on observed calibration tasks—a stronger guarantee than prior meta-conformal prediction work, which only provides marginal coverage guarantees averaged over task distributions. The algorithm operates in two stages: first constructing PAC prediction sets for each calibration task, then building a prediction interval over these parameters. Experiments on mini-ImageNet, CIFAR10-C, FewRel, and CDC Heart Dataset demonstrate that Meta-PS satisfies its theoretical guarantees while producing smaller prediction sets than conservative baselines.

## Strengths

**1. Well-motivated theoretical contribution.** The paper addresses a genuine gap in meta-learning uncertainty quantification. As the authors clearly articulate, the distinction between task-marginal guarantees (existing meta-conformal prediction) and task-conditional guarantees (this work) is important for practical deployment. The mPAC definition (Definition 3) meaningfully extends standard PAC prediction sets to handle three sources of randomness hierarchically rather than treating them uniformly. Figure 2 effectively communicates this distinction through graphical models, making the conceptual advantage over prior work immediately clear. The motivation that a single fixed calibration set can be reused across many future tasks (rather than requiring recalibration for each task) is particularly compelling for practical applications.

**2. Sound theoretical framework with careful treatment of randomness.** Theorem 1 is proven correctly, and the authors deserve credit for recognizing that computing PAC-thresholds for each calibration task does not require a union bound factor of 1/N. The insight that we only need the output of the PAC method to be i.i.d. (not each individual threshold to be valid simultaneously) allows the authors to avoid this inefficiency in favor of two 1/2 factors—a considerably more efficient approach. The complete proofs and careful statement of assumptions strengthen the theoretical contribution.

**3. Practical algorithm design with broad applicability.** Algorithm 1 is elegantly simple and agnostic to the underlying meta-learning method, allowing use of any score function as a subroutine. The two-step approach (meta calibration followed by parameter interval construction) is intuitive and modular, making the method broadly applicable across different meta-learning paradigms.

**4. Comprehensive experimental validation across domains.** Experiments span three distinct domains (vision, NLP, healthcare) with diverse datasets and multiple trials (100 calibration trials × 50 test trials), demonstrating that Meta-PS empirically satisfies its theoretical guarantees. The choice to include a realistic medical application (CDC Heart Dataset stratified by year and state) strengthens the practical relevance of the work.

**5. Clear and generally well-written presentation.** The paper does a good job communicating the problem setup and high-level intuition, with effective use of graphical models to illustrate conceptual differences.

## Weaknesses

**1. Presentation and notation could be significantly improved.** While the paper is generally well-written, the notation is quite burdensome, particularly in Section 4. The paper would benefit substantially from a high-level, step-by-step description of what the authors aim to accomplish before diving into technical details. For example, plainly stating upfront that Meta-PAC sets aim to find a single shared threshold τ such that:
   - On average over the random draw of calibration tasks used to choose τ...
   - and on average over the random choice of test task...
   - and on average over predictions the model makes on the new task...
   - the prediction set contains the correct label (with high probability 1−δ, 1−α, 1−ε)...

would make the subsequent technical development much easier to follow. Additionally, lines 171-174 are difficult to parse; the role of g and the addition of the dummy label require clarification. The multiple uses of f notation (f, f^A, f^{Ai}) could be simplified.

**2. Limited algorithmic novelty.** While the theoretical extension to meta-learning is valuable, the core algorithm essentially applies existing PAC prediction set techniques hierarchically—first to individual tasks, then to the distribution of parameters. The proof strategy for Theorem 1 appears to be a relatively straightforward application of existing techniques to this hierarchical setting. The individual components (Algorithm 2 for PAC calibration) are standard, and while their combination is novel for meta-learning, the algorithmic contribution is somewhat incremental.

**3. Critical practical limitations under-explored.** The requirement for N calibration tasks and n calibration examples per task is substantial. The experiments use N=500 and n=2500 for mini-ImageNet, which is quite large. However, the paper provides:
   - No concrete guidance on how many calibration tasks and examples are needed for different problem sizes and desired guarantee levels (ε, α, δ)
   - No sensitivity analysis showing how performance degrades when these quantities are insufficient
   - No analysis of sample complexity relating δ, α, ε to N, n, t
   - Limited discussion of computational complexity—the two-step procedure requires running PAC prediction set construction N times, which could be expensive

These omissions are problematic for practitioners trying to apply the method in resource-constrained settings. The paper cites Park et al. (2020) on calibration requirements but doesn't provide domain-specific guidance.

**4. Restrictive assumptions not thoroughly discussed.** The algorithm assumes calibration and test tasks are exchangeable samples from p_π i.i.d., which may not hold in practice. For example:
   - Geographic regions in autonomous driving may have correlated weather patterns
   - Healthcare data may exhibit temporal distribution shift across years
   - The paper doesn't analyze robustness to task distribution mismatch or provide empirical validation under slight distribution shifts

The exchangeability assumption should be stated more prominently as a limitation. Additionally, the paper doesn't discuss how violations of this assumption would affect the guarantee or provide graceful degradation analysis.

**5. Fixed threshold across tasks is inflexible.** A bothersome aspect is that a single threshold τ is used for all downstream tasks and never updates, regardless of the size of the adaptation set A or apparent task difficulty. In contrast, Meta-CP attempts to estimate a data-dependent threshold (the adjusted quantile prediction). When there is sufficient few-shot data or significant variability across tasks, Meta-CP's approach could be better in terms of test-task conditional performance. The paper doesn't analyze when this limitation becomes problematic or compare computational costs fairly.

**6. Limited experimental baselines and sensitivity analysis.** 
   - Meta-CP is the only meta-learning baseline; comparisons with other uncertainty quantification approaches in meta-learning (Bayesian methods, ensemble approaches) would strengthen the evaluation
   - All experiments use only prototypical networks; generalization to other meta-learning algorithms (MAML, Relation Networks, etc.) is unclear
   - No ablation studies on key design choices (e.g., why split δ as δ/2 in Algorithm 1 rather than other allocations?)
   - Parameter choices (δ = 10^-5, α = 0.1, ε = 0.1) appear arbitrary with no sensitivity analysis or guidance for practitioners
   - The comparison with PS-Test uses different numbers of test examples (20 shots vs. 5 for adaptation), making the comparison somewhat unfair
   - Figure 3c shows Meta-CP failing to control error, but the paper doesn't clarify whether this is due to fundamental limitations or implementation/tuning issues

**7. Tight coupling to score function quality.** The guarantee holds given a fixed score function f, but doesn't account for potential poor meta-learning. The paper doesn't analyze how prediction set size degrades if f is poorly trained or provide methods to detect when the score function is inadequate. Additionally, the choice of score function g(τ,y) = τ·𝟙(y=1) lacks justification—have alternatives been considered? How sensitive are results to this choice?

**8. Theoretical-practical gap on Clopper-Pearson bounds.** How tight are Clopper-Pearson based bounds when using small calibration task sizes? This is relevant given the sample complexity concerns raised above. While the theory is technically solid, it represents a relatively straightforward combination of previous techniques (Meta-CP and PAC calibration), suggesting only modest practical impact.

## Questions and Suggestions

1. **Sample complexity guidance**: Can you provide concrete guidance on how N and n should scale with δ, α, ε, and |Y|? Practitioners need to know how much calibration

reviews.text:
# Hybrid Peer Review: PAC Prediction Sets for Meta-Learning

## Summary

This paper proposes Meta-PS, an algorithm for constructing Probably Approximately Correct (PAC) prediction sets in the meta-learning setting. The core contribution is extending PAC prediction set guarantees to handle three sources of randomness simultaneously: calibration data, test task adaptation sets, and test evaluation data. The authors introduce a meta-PAC (mPAC) guarantee that holds with high probability over future tasks, which represents a meaningful advancement over existing meta-conformal prediction approaches that only provide average-case guarantees across tasks. The algorithm operates in two stages: first constructing PAC prediction sets for each calibration task using adapted score functions, then building a prediction interval over these parameters. Experiments on four datasets spanning three domains (vision via mini-ImageNet and CIFAR10-C, NLP via FewRel, and healthcare via CDC Heart Dataset) demonstrate that Meta-PS satisfies its theoretical guarantees while producing smaller prediction sets compared to baselines.

## Strengths

**1. Well-motivated theoretical contribution.** The paper addresses a genuine gap in meta-learning uncertainty quantification. The distinction between task-marginal guarantees (provided by existing work) and task-conditional guarantees (this work) is important for safety-critical applications where practitioners need guarantees that hold for each individual future task, not just on average. The meta-PAC definition (Definition 3) is clearly articulated and meaningfully extends classical PAC prediction sets to the meta-learning setting, with finer-grained control over randomness sources. Figure 2 effectively communicates this distinction through graphical models, making the conceptual advance intuitive.

**2. Solid theoretical framework.** Theorem 1 is proven correctly and the paper provides complete proofs with carefully stated assumptions. The graphical model comparison clarifies why the proposed three-parameter guarantee is superior to prior approaches for the stated use cases. The exchangeability assumption, while strong, is explicitly stated in the problem formulation.

**3. Practical algorithm design.** Algorithm 1 is elegantly simple and modular—it reuses existing PAC prediction set algorithms as subroutines, making the approach broadly applicable across different meta-learning paradigms. The two-step approach (meta calibration via PAC prediction sets on each task, then parameter interval construction) is intuitive and well-justified. Importantly, the method reuses the same parameter τ across adaptation without requiring additional calibration on test tasks, which is computationally efficient compared to approaches that would require per-task recalibration.

**4. Comprehensive experimental validation.** Experiments span three domains (vision, NLP, healthcare) with appropriate baselines and evaluation metrics aligned with the theoretical guarantees. The empirical validation of the three-parameter guarantee is convincing, with proper statistical reporting using box plots and multiple trials (100 calibration trials × 50 test trials). The experiments consistently demonstrate that Meta-PS satisfies its guarantees while producing smaller prediction sets than conservative baselines.

**5. Clear presentation.** The paper is generally well-written with clear motivation and good exposition of the problem setup. The use of graphical models to illustrate conceptual differences and the careful development of the problem formulation make the work accessible.

## Weaknesses

**1. Limited algorithmic novelty.** While the theoretical extension to meta-learning is valuable, the algorithmic contribution is relatively straightforward. The core technique essentially applies existing PAC prediction set methods hierarchically—first to individual tasks, then to the distribution of parameters. The key insight that task parameters are i.i.d. across calibration tasks is natural given the problem structure, and the algorithm amounts to running a standard PAC algorithm twice. The individual components (Algorithm 2 is standard) are not new, though their combination for meta-learning is novel.

**2. Substantial data requirements without sufficient practical guidance.** The algorithm requires N calibration tasks and n calibration examples per task. For mini-ImageNet experiments, this translates to N=500 and n=2500, which is quite substantial. While the authors acknowledge this limitation and cite Park et al. (2020) for sample complexity analysis, the paper provides no concrete guidance for practitioners on how many calibration tasks and examples are needed for various problem sizes and desired guarantee levels (ε, α, δ). Critically, there is no empirical sensitivity analysis showing how prediction set size and error degrade when N and n are smaller than tested. For practitioners, guidance on selecting these parameters would be valuable. Additionally, the paper does not provide finite-sample complexity bounds relating δ, α, ε to N, n, and t, which would help practitioners determine required calibration data.

**3. Limited experimental scope and baseline comparisons.** The evaluation has several gaps:
   - Meta-CP is the only meta-learning baseline compared; other uncertainty quantification approaches in meta-learning (Bayesian methods, ensemble approaches) are absent. The PS and PS-Test baselines are somewhat strawman comparisons.
   - All experiments use prototypical networks as the meta-learning algorithm; generalization to other approaches (MAML, Relation Networks, memory-augmented methods) is unclear. This is a significant limitation since the paper claims to be agnostic to the underlying meta-learning method.
   - For mini-ImageNet, only 5-way classification is tested. Evaluation on more challenging settings (10-way or 20-way) would strengthen claims about the method's practical utility.
   - No ablation studies on key design choices. For example, why is the parameter δ split equally as δ/2 in both steps of Algorithm 1? Is this optimal, or would an unequal split improve prediction set size?
   - The CDC Heart experiment uses only t=10 adaptation examples, which may be insufficient for reliable task adaptation. Sensitivity analysis across different values of t would be informative.
   - Parameter choices (δ = 10^-5, α = 0.1, ε = 0.1) appear somewhat arbitrary with no sensitivity analysis or justification for these specific values.

**4. Strong assumptions with limited robustness analysis.** The algorithm assumes task distributions are i.i.d. samples from p_π, and that calibration and test tasks are exchangeable. These assumptions may not hold in practice:
   - Geographic regions in autonomous driving may have correlated weather patterns
   - Temporal distribution shift could occur between calibration and deployment periods
   - Hierarchical task structures might violate exchangeability
   
   The paper briefly mentions the autonomous driving example but doesn't explore robustness to task distribution shift. No empirical validation under slight distribution mismatch is provided. The exchangeability assumption should be stated more prominently as a limitation, and the paper should discuss when it may fail in practice.

**5. Incomplete analysis of practical considerations.** Several important practical aspects are underexplored:
   - **Computational cost**: No discussion of computational complexity. The two-stage algorithm requires running PAC prediction set construction N times, which could be expensive. Wall-clock time comparisons with Meta-CP would be valuable.
   - **Score function quality**: The guarantee depends critically on the meta-learned score function f being well-adapted. The paper does not analyze how meta-learning quality affects the final guarantee or prediction set size. How does poor meta-learning degrade the guarantee?
   - **Reuse of τ after adaptation**: While computationally efficient, the validity of reusing the same parameter τ across different test tasks (Section 4.2) relies on the assumption that the adaptation set A is representative and tasks are exchangeable. For very small t, this may fail. Empirical analysis of performance as t varies would strengthen the claim that this is valid.
   - **Scalability with label classes**: The paper does not discuss how prediction set size scales with the number of possible labels or how the method would perform on problems with large label spaces.

**6. Presentation issues.** The notation is dense, particularly in Section 4.1, with multiple uses of f, f^A, f^{Ai} that could be simplified. Algorithm 1 references Algorithm 2 (PS-BINOM) in the appendix, but the main paper doesn't explain what PS-BINOM does, making Algorithm 1 difficult to understand standalone. The relationship between the score function f and the prediction set construction could be explained more intuitively before diving into technical details.

**7. Limited exploration of when the guarantee matters.** While the paper motivates the need for task-conditional guarantees, the experiments don't deeply investigate scenarios where Meta-CP's task-marginal guarantee would be problematic in practice. Figure 3c and 6 provide some evidence, but the paper doesn't clearly explain why Meta-CP fails empirically—is this a fundamental limitation of the method or an implementation/tuning issue? More detailed analysis would strengthen the case for the proposed approach.

## Questions and Suggestions

1. **Sample complexity and practical guidance**: Can you provide finite-sample complexity bounds characterizing how N, n must scale with δ, α, ε? This is crucial for practitioners. Additionally, how do prediction set error and size vary with N, n, and t? Plots showing these trade-offs across a range of values would be

Summary:
This paper proposes a PAC prediction set algorithm for meta learning. The goal is to adapt the predictor to new unseen tasks with a few labeled training examples. Experiments on four datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed methods with  PAC guarantee while having smaller prediction set size.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strengths:


* The paper is well-written.
* The proposed method is reasonable with theoretical analysis.
* Experiments on various domain and datasets are compared.


Weakness:



* For reusing the same meta prediction set parameter after adaptation, would be better if the meta predictions set parameters are also adapted?
* The current methods seem only applies on metric-based meta-learning methods, e.g., prototypical network. It would be better if the methods could be also applies on optimization-based methods, such as model-agnostic meta learning (MAML).
* For mini-ImageNet datasets, only 5-way classification problem is compared. It would be better if the authors can compare on more challenging, 10-way or 20-way classification.

Questions:
Please refer to weaknesss above.

Limitations:
Yes, the authors have addressed the limitations and potential negative societal impact of their work.

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

reviews.summary: This paper considers the notions and algorithms for generating a prediction set under the meta learning setting that satisfies PAC guarantees. Since there are multiple distributions involved in meta learning setting, the considered PAC notion for meta learning is different from existing notion designed for conventional machine learning setting, such as [3]. Furthermore, the meta learning setting is also different from covariate shift case considered in [6]. For example, adaptation data sets are available for a trained model to adapt these associated distributions without estimation of importance weights. Therefore, meta-PAC (mPAC) notion is proposed (Definition 3), which uses an augmented calibration set from N distributions, i.e., $p_{\theta_1}, …, p_{\theta_N}$ to generate $\varepsilon$-correct prediction set for a testing distribution $p_\theta$ whp $1-\delta$. Algorithm 1 is proposed to determine the parameter $\tau$ of prediction set, which uses a two-step strategy to first determine ($\varepsilon, \alpha/2$)-PAC estimator/threshold on each of N distributions, and then use these thresholds (with a label 1 attached to each threshold) to construct a new sample of size N to determine a threshold with ($\alpha/2, \delta$)-PAC. Theorem 1 shows the guarantee of this algorithm for ($\varepsilon, \alpha, \delta$)-mPAC.

Summary:
This paper considers the notions and algorithms for generating a prediction set under the meta learning setting that satisfies PAC guarantees. Since there are multiple distributions involved in meta learning setting, the considered PAC notion for meta learning is different from existing notion designed for conventional machine learning setting, such as [3]. Furthermore, the meta learning setting is also different from covariate shift case considered in [6]. For example, adaptation data sets are available for a trained model to adapt these associated distributions without estimation of importance weights. Therefore, meta-PAC (mPAC) notion is proposed (Definition 3), which uses an augmented calibration set from N distributions, i.e., $p_{\theta_1}, …, p_{\theta_N}$ to generate $\varepsilon$-correct prediction set for a testing distribution $p_\theta$ whp $1-\delta$. Algorithm 1 is proposed to determine the parameter $\tau$ of prediction set, which uses a two-step strategy to first determine ($\varepsilon, \alpha/2$)-PAC estimator/threshold on each of N distributions, and then use these thresholds (with a label 1 attached to each threshold) to construct a new sample of size N to determine a threshold with ($\alpha/2, \delta$)-PAC. Theorem 1 shows the guarantee of this algorithm for ($\varepsilon, \alpha, \delta$)-mPAC.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Originality:
This paper generalizes PAC notation of conformal prediction from previous studies to mPAC in meta learning case, where multiple distributions are present, augmented calibration set is available. Algorithm is proposed to guarantee mPAC built on the existing algorithm designed for PAC prediction. 

Quality:
This paper is technically sound and theoretical analysis is clear.

Clarity:
This paper is easy to follow.

Significance: 
This paper provides insights to conformal prediction in meta learning setting.

Questions:
Algorithm 2 involves grid search for $\tau$. Could the authors elaborate a bit more on its computational cost/efficiency in analysis or experiment (e.g., the Granularity for searching)? Would there be any possible way to accelerate such grid search?

Limitations:
I think the authors adequately addressed the limitations and potential negative societal impact of their work

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
# Hybrid Peer Review

## Summary

This paper proposes Meta-PS, an algorithm for constructing Probably Approximately Correct (PAC) prediction sets in the meta-learning setting. The work extends the classical PAC prediction set framework to handle the hierarchical structure of meta-learning, where multiple task distributions are present and adaptation data is available for test tasks. The key contribution is the introduction of a meta-PAC (mPAC) guarantee (Definition 3) that controls error over three sources of randomness: calibration data, test task adaptation sets, and test evaluation data. This is a meaningful departure from existing meta-conformal prediction approaches, which provide only task-marginal guarantees averaged across tasks. Algorithm 1 employs a two-step strategy: first determining (ε, α/2)-PAC estimators on each of N calibration distributions, then using these thresholds to construct a new sample to determine a final threshold with (α/2, δ)-PAC guarantees. Theorem 1 establishes the correctness of this approach, and experiments across multiple domains (vision, NLP, healthcare) demonstrate that Meta-PS produces smaller prediction sets while satisfying the mPAC guarantee compared to baselines.

## Strengths

**Well-motivated theoretical contribution**: The paper addresses a genuine and important gap in meta-learning uncertainty quantification. The distinction between task-marginal guarantees (existing work) and task-conditional guarantees (this work) is crucial for safety-critical applications where we need guarantees that hold for individual test tasks rather than only in expectation across tasks. Figure 2 effectively communicates this distinction through graphical models, making the conceptual advance clear and intuitive.

**Technically sound analysis**: The paper is mathematically rigorous with complete proofs. The theoretical framework is clearly presented, with careful statement of assumptions (exchangeability of task distributions, i.i.d. calibration examples). Theorem 1 is proven correctly, and the algorithm's two-stage design—meta calibration via PAC prediction sets on each task, then constructing a prediction interval over parameters—is elegant and well-justified.

**Practical algorithm design**: Algorithm 1 is appealingly simple and modular, allowing it to serve as a wrapper around any existing PAC prediction set algorithm (such as PS-BINOM). This generality makes the approach broadly applicable across different meta-learning settings without requiring fundamental changes to the core algorithm.

**Comprehensive experimental validation**: The evaluation spans three distinct domains (vision with mini-ImageNet and CIFAR10-C, NLP with FewRel, and healthcare with CDC Heart Dataset), with appropriate statistical reporting using box plots and multiple trials (100 calibration trials × 50 test trials). The experiments provide convincing empirical validation that the method satisfies its theoretical guarantees while producing smaller prediction sets than conservative baselines.

**Clear presentation**: The paper is generally well-written with effective use of graphical models to illustrate conceptual differences and clear exposition of the problem setup and motivation.

## Weaknesses

**Limited algorithmic novelty**: While the theoretical extension to meta-learning is valuable, the core algorithmic contribution is relatively straightforward. The approach essentially applies existing PAC prediction set techniques hierarchically—running a PAC algorithm on each calibration task, then running it again on the resulting parameters. The individual components are not new, and the combination, while sound, is a natural application of existing techniques to a hierarchical setting. The proof strategy in Theorem 1 appears to be a relatively direct application of standard PAC prediction set analysis rather than introducing fundamentally new proof techniques.

**Insufficient experimental analysis and sensitivity**: The experimental evaluation, while comprehensive in scope, lacks depth in several critical dimensions:
- No ablation studies on key design choices. For instance, the paper splits the error budget equally (δ/2 in each step of Algorithm 1) but provides no justification for this allocation or analysis of whether alternative splits (e.g., δ/3 and 2δ/3) would improve prediction set size.
- Missing sensitivity analysis: how do results vary with N (number of calibration tasks), n (calibration examples per task), and t (adaptation set size)? Only single configurations are tested per dataset, making it difficult to understand the method's behavior across different practical scenarios.
- Limited baseline comparisons: Meta-CP is the only directly comparable meta-learning baseline. Comparisons with other uncertainty quantification approaches in meta-learning (e.g., Bayesian methods, ensemble approaches) would substantially strengthen the evaluation.
- The comparison with Meta-CP deserves clarification: Figure 3c shows Meta-CP failing to control error over test tasks, but it's unclear whether this reflects a fundamental limitation or suboptimal hyperparameter tuning.

**Substantial practical limitations under-explored**: 
- The algorithm requires N calibration tasks and n calibration examples per task. The paper acknowledges this limitation but provides minimal practical guidance. For mini-ImageNet experiments, N=500 and n=2500 is quite large. The paper cites Park et al. (2020) for sample complexity analysis but does not provide concrete guidance on how many calibration tasks or examples are "enough" for practitioners, nor does it characterize how N, n, t must scale with δ, α, ε.
- Computational complexity is not discussed. The two-step procedure requires running PAC prediction set construction N times, which could be expensive. Wall-clock time comparisons with Meta-CP would clarify practical efficiency.
- All experiments use prototypical networks as the score function; generalization to other meta-learning algorithms (MAML, Relation Networks, etc.) remains unclear. It's uncertain whether the guarantee holds independently of the meta-learning approach.

**Strong assumptions with limited robustness analysis**: The approach assumes task distributions are i.i.d. samples from p_π (exchangeability assumption), which may not hold in practice. For example, geographical regions in autonomous driving may have correlated weather patterns, or temporal distribution shift could occur between calibration and deployment periods. While the paper briefly mentions this limitation in motivating examples, it provides no empirical validation of robustness to task distribution mismatch or analysis of how the guarantee degrades under assumption violations. The paper should discuss when these assumptions are most/least likely to hold.

**Incomplete treatment of critical limitations**: 
- The requirement that the same parameter τ be reused after task adaptation (Section 4.2) assumes the adaptation set A is representative of the test distribution. For very small t (e.g., t=10 in the CDC Heart experiment), this assumption may fail. The paper lacks formal justification and empirical analysis of performance as t varies.
- The guarantee depends critically on the quality of the meta-learned score function f. Poor meta-learning would lead to poor prediction sets, but the paper does not analyze how meta-learning quality affects the final guarantee or provide guidance on detecting when f is poorly trained.
- Prediction set size may grow prohibitively with the number of possible labels. The paper does not discuss scalability to problems with large label spaces or open-set scenarios where test tasks may contain unseen classes.

**Presentation issues**: 
- The notation is dense (particularly Section 4.1), with multiple uses of f, f^A, f^{Ai} that could be simplified. 
- Algorithm 1 references Algorithm 2 (PS-BINOM) in the appendix, but the main paper does not explain what PS-BINOM does, making Algorithm 1 difficult to understand standalone.
- Some design choices lack clear justification. For example, why is the score function g defined as g(τ,y) = τ·𝟙(y=1) specifically? Have alternatives been considered?

**Baseline fairness concerns**: The comparison with PS-Test uses 20 shots from the test distribution, while Meta-PS uses only 5 shots for adaptation, making the comparison somewhat asymmetric. A fair comparison would use the same number of test examples across methods.

## Questions and Suggestions

1. **Algorithm design and optimality**: Why is the parameter δ split equally as δ/2 in both steps (Algorithm 1, Lines 3 and 5)? Is this allocation optimal, or would an unequal split improve prediction set size? An ablation study would clarify this design choice.

2. **Finite-sample complexity**: Can you provide concrete finite-sample complexity bounds characterizing how N, n should scale with δ, α, ε, and |Y| to guarantee the meta-PAC property? This is crucial for practitioners determining required calibration data.

3. **Sensitivity and scalability analysis**: Please provide empirical analysis of how prediction set error and size vary across ranges of N, n, and t. How does the method perform with smaller calibration sets? How does prediction set size scale with the number of classes?

4. **Robustness to assumption violations**: How does the guarantee degrade if task distributions are not exactly i.i.d. from p_π? Can you provide empirical validation under slight task distribution mismatch (e.g., correlated or temporally shifted tasks)?

5. **Meta-learning algorithm dependence**: How does performance vary with other meta-learning algorithms (MAML, Relation Networks, etc

Summary:
This paper considers the notions and algorithms for generating a prediction set under the meta learning setting that satisfies PAC guarantees. Since there are multiple distributions involved in meta learning setting, the considered PAC notion for meta learning is different from existing notion designed for conventional machine learning setting, such as [3]. Furthermore, the meta learning setting is also different from covariate shift case considered in [6]. For example, adaptation data sets are available for a trained model to adapt these associated distributions without estimation of importance weights. Therefore, meta-PAC (mPAC) notion is proposed (Definition 3), which uses an augmented calibration set from N distributions, i.e., $p_{\theta_1}, …, p_{\theta_N}$ to generate $\varepsilon$-correct prediction set for a testing distribution $p_\theta$ whp $1-\delta$. Algorithm 1 is proposed to determine the parameter $\tau$ of prediction set, which uses a two-step strategy to first determine ($\varepsilon, \alpha/2$)-PAC estimator/threshold on each of N distributions, and then use these thresholds (with a label 1 attached to each threshold) to construct a new sample of size N to determine a threshold with ($\alpha/2, \delta$)-PAC. Theorem 1 shows the guarantee of this algorithm for ($\varepsilon, \alpha, \delta$)-mPAC.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Originality:
This paper generalizes PAC notation of conformal prediction from previous studies to mPAC in meta learning case, where multiple distributions are present, augmented calibration set is available. Algorithm is proposed to guarantee mPAC built on the existing algorithm designed for PAC prediction. 

Quality:
This paper is technically sound and theoretical analysis is clear.

Clarity:
This paper is easy to follow.

Significance: 
This paper provides insights to conformal prediction in meta learning setting.

Questions:
Algorithm 2 involves grid search for $\tau$. Could the authors elaborate a bit more on its computational cost/efficiency in analysis or experiment (e.g., the Granularity for searching)? Would there be any possible way to accelerate such grid search?

Limitations:
I think the authors adequately addressed the limitations and potential negative societal impact of their work

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

reviews.summary:
POST DISCUSSION:

The authors addressed most of my issues about readability and the attribution problem, so I have increased my score to a weak accept.

My final assessment is as follows:
I believe the proofs are correct, and the related work is correct.
I think the paper is the first to do a two-stage training-conditional guarantee, which has some value. Although this guarantee is a straightforward extension of existing results, perhaps somebody might be interested in it, and I do not believe it has appeared before.

I also believe it contributes by being the second paper to use training-conditional conformal for meta-learning. It is slightly different from the existing work by Adam Fisch et al. in that it also conditions on the tasks. Maybe somebody will find that useful!

_____________
This paper introduces a form of distribution-free uncertainty quantification guarantee into meta-learning and proposes an algorithm to achieve it. The guarantee is a triply-nested one, where the innermost integral is an expectation on a 0-1 loss. This expectation is nested within two other probabilities, conditioned on the calibration set and the meta learned parameters.  The algorithm boils down to two applications of binomial concentration. There are then extensive experiments on several meta learning tasks in vision, language, and medicine.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths: I the technical results are clearly correct. The experiments are comprehensive.

Weaknesses:

(1) The guarantee provided in this paper is so complicated to interpret that I worry it will never be used.  It is already difficult enough to interpret a tolerance interval, and adding an extra parameter incurs such a high cognitive cost for the user that one may never know how to set these parameters.

(2) The paper is difficult to understand, as the writing is dense and exceedingly complicated. For example, take Figure 2. It includes a lot of extraneous information, the colors are missing, and certain aspects of the plot are not explained. What do the orientations of the stripes mean, for example? What does the box with “N” mean? Another part that is hard to read is 4.1.  There is just too much going on and I’m not sure anybody except for a very small community working on these PAC type guarantees will understand it.

(3) The technical result is not too interesting and the domain area is not novel.  The meta conformal prediction paper already provides a guarantee in this problem domain that works well, although it is marginal on the meta learned parameters. The fact that we can apply binomial concentration twice as opposed to once in order to condition again on those variables is basically an extension of existing results. Having said that, I think it is interesting and clever that we can chain the concentration argument twice. Nice work on the algorithm. 

(4) I believe there is a major attribution issue throughout the work. The idea of training-conditional prediction sets was first proposed by Vovk [21]. Rebranding it as PAC prediction sets and citing both works as the source of the idea is wrong. The idea comes from Vovk and his paper should get all of the credit for that.  Please remove all instances of the following attribution: “PAC prediction sets [3]”. The same is true of the so-called PS-Binom algorithm, which is essentially the same as that of Vovk in Proposition 2b of [21].  The paper [3] can be credited for applying [21] in a deep learning setting, but [21] should be cited as the sole inventor of this idea, in this and any future work. I also take some issue with the rebranding as PAC prediction sets as opposed to training-conditional validity, as they are exactly equivalent and the latter terminology came first. I will argue for rejection of the paper unless these attributions are amended.
reviews.questions: What does it mean for tasks to be “iid” in the real world, outside of synthetic setups? What are some realistic settings where we might expect the guarantee provided from the authors to actually hold?
reviews.limitations: I believe the limitations have been addressed. (I do not believe there are any serious ethical limitations of this paper in the first place, and ethics review should certainly not get in the way of the publication of this paper.)

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strengths: I the technical results are clearly correct. The experiments are comprehensive.

Weaknesses:

(1) The guarantee provided in this paper is so complicated to interpret that I worry it will never be used.  It is already difficult enough to interpret a tolerance interval, and adding an extra parameter incurs such a high cognitive cost for the user that one may never know how to set these parameters.

(2) The paper is difficult to understand, as the writing is dense and exceedingly complicated. For example, take Figure 2. It includes a lot of extraneous information, the colors are missing, and certain aspects of the plot are not explained. What do the orientations of the stripes mean, for example? What does the box with “N” mean? Another part that is hard to read is 4.1.  There is just too much going on and I’m not sure anybody except for a very small community working on these PAC type guarantees will understand it.

The meta conformal prediction paper already provides a guarantee in this problem domain that works well, although it is marginal on the meta learned parameters. The fact that we can apply binomial concentration twice as opposed to once in order to condition again on those variables is basically an extension of existing results. Having said that, I think it is interesting and clever that we can chain the concentration argument twice. Nice work on the algorithm. 

(4) I believe there is a major attribution issue throughout the work. The idea of training-conditional prediction sets was first proposed by Vovk [21]. Rebranding it as PAC prediction sets and citing both works as the source of the idea is wrong. The idea comes from Vovk and his paper should get all of the credit for that.  Please remove all instances of the following attribution: “PAC prediction sets [3]”. The same is true of the so-called PS-Binom algorithm, which is essentially the same as that of Vovk in Proposition 2b of [21].  The paper [3] can be credited for applying [21] in a deep learning setting, but [21] should be cited as the sole inventor of this idea, in this and any future work. I also take some issue with the rebranding as PAC prediction sets as opposed to training-conditional validity, as they are exactly equivalent and the latter terminology came first. I will argue for rejection of the paper unless these attributions are amended.

Questions:
What does it mean for tasks to be “iid” in the real world, outside of synthetic setups? What are some realistic settings where we might expect the guarantee provided from the authors to actually hold?

Limitations:
I believe the limitations have been addressed. (I do not believe there are any serious ethical limitations of this paper in the first place, and ethics review should certainly not get in the way of the publication of this paper.)

6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
# Hybrid Peer Review

## Summary

This paper introduces Meta-PS, an algorithm for constructing Probably Approximately Correct (PAC) prediction sets in meta-learning settings. The key contribution is extending PAC prediction sets to provide a triply-nested guarantee—a meta-PAC (mPAC) guarantee—that conditions on three sources of randomness: calibration data, test task adaptation sets, and test evaluation data. This is stronger than existing work (meta-conformal prediction) which only provides task-marginal guarantees. The algorithm operates in two stages: first constructing PAC prediction sets for each calibration task, then building a prediction interval over these parameters. The approach is validated on four datasets spanning vision (mini-ImageNet, CIFAR10-C), language (FewRel), and healthcare (CDC Heart Dataset) domains.

## Strengths

1. **Solid theoretical contribution with clear problem motivation**: The paper addresses a genuine gap in meta-learning uncertainty quantification. The distinction between task-marginal guarantees (existing work) and task-conditional guarantees (this work) is well-articulated and important for safety-critical applications. Figure 2 effectively communicates this conceptual difference through graphical models, making the contribution intuitive. The mPAC definition (Definition 3) carefully controls three sources of randomness rather than treating them uniformly, representing a meaningful extension of standard PAC prediction sets.

2. **Correct technical results**: The proofs are sound, and the paper is the first to achieve a two-stage training-conditional guarantee in this problem domain. While the extension is relatively straightforward—essentially applying binomial concentration twice rather than once—the chaining of the concentration argument is clever. The algorithm is elegantly simple and modular, allowing use of any PAC prediction set algorithm as a subroutine, which enhances its practical applicability.

3. **Comprehensive experimental validation**: Experiments span multiple domains with appropriate statistical reporting (box plots, 100 calibration trials × 50 test trials). The empirical validation that Meta-PS satisfies its theoretical guarantees while producing smaller prediction sets than baselines is convincing. The diverse application domains (vision, NLP, healthcare) strengthen the generality claims.

4. **Practical algorithm design**: Algorithm 1 is intuitive and reuses existing PAC prediction set algorithms as black-box components. The two-step procedure (meta calibration followed by parameter interval construction) is straightforward to implement and understand at a high level.

## Major Weaknesses

1. **Interpretability and practical utility of the guarantee**: The guarantee provided is so complicated to interpret that it raises serious concerns about whether it will ever be used in practice. The triply-nested guarantee with three parameters (ε, α, δ) imposes a high cognitive cost on users who must understand and set these parameters appropriately. Beyond the inherent difficulty of interpreting tolerance intervals, adding the extra layer of meta-learning parameters makes the guarantee nearly impenetrable to practitioners. This complexity threatens the practical impact of the work, as users may struggle to determine appropriate parameter settings or understand when the guarantee actually holds.

2. **Attribution issues**: There is a major attribution problem throughout the work. The idea of training-conditional prediction sets was first proposed by Vovk [21], not by the cited PAC prediction set literature. The paper incorrectly attributes this idea to [3] and rebrand it as "PAC prediction sets," when the terminology "training-conditional validity" came first and is exactly equivalent. Similarly, the PS-Binom algorithm is essentially identical to Vovk's Proposition 2b in [21]. While [3] should be credited for applying Vovk's ideas in deep learning, [21] must be cited as the sole inventor of the core idea. All instances of "PAC prediction sets [3]" should be removed and replaced with proper attribution to Vovk. This attribution error must be corrected before publication.

3. **Dense presentation and readability**: The paper is difficult to understand due to dense writing and excessive complexity. Section 4.1 is particularly problematic, with too much technical content compressed together that obscures rather than clarifies. Figure 2, while attempting to convey important conceptual differences, includes extraneous information, missing color specifications, and unexplained visual elements (e.g., what do stripe orientations mean? What does the box labeled "N" represent?). The notation is heavy throughout, with multiple uses of f, f^A, f^{Ai} that could be simplified. Algorithm 1 references Algorithm 2 (PS-BINOM) without explaining what it does, making the main algorithm difficult to understand standalone. The paper's density and complexity will likely limit its audience to a small community working on PAC-type guarantees, significantly reducing its potential impact.

## Moderate Weaknesses

4. **Limited algorithmic novelty**: The core contribution amounts to applying existing PAC prediction set techniques hierarchically—running a PAC algorithm on calibration tasks, then running it again on the resulting parameters. While the theoretical extension to meta-learning is valuable, the algorithmic novelty is limited. The insight that parameters are i.i.d. across tasks is natural given the problem structure, and the two-stage approach follows straightforwardly from this observation.

5. **Insufficient exploration of practical limitations**: 
   - **Calibration data requirements**: The algorithm requires N calibration tasks with n calibration examples each (e.g., N=500, n=2500 for mini-ImageNet experiments). This is substantial and acknowledged only briefly. The paper lacks concrete guidance on how many calibration tasks are "enough" for practitioners. No empirical analysis is provided on how performance degrades with smaller N and n, nor is there sensitivity analysis across different problem sizes and desired guarantee levels (ε, α, δ).
   - **Exchangeability assumptions**: The method assumes calibration and test tasks are exchangeable samples from p_π, which may not hold in practice. The paper briefly mentions this limitation (e.g., geographic regions in autonomous driving may have correlated weather patterns) but doesn't explore robustness to task distribution mismatch. Empirical validation under distribution shift between calibration and deployment would strengthen the work.
   - **Adaptation set sensitivity**: The method reuses the same τ parameter across different test tasks without recalibration. While this is computationally efficient, the paper doesn't thoroughly analyze sensitivity to adaptation set size t or provide guidance on selecting t. Using only t=10 examples in the CDC Heart experiment raises questions about whether adaptation is sufficient.

6. **Limited experimental baselines and analysis**:
   - Meta-CP is the only meta-learning baseline; comparison with other uncertainty quantification approaches in meta-learning (Bayesian methods, ensemble approaches) is absent.
   - All experiments use prototypical networks as the score function; generalization to other meta-learning algorithms (MAML, Relation Networks, etc.) remains unclear.
   - The comparison with Meta-CP may not be entirely fair—Meta-CP trains an additional quantile predictor, and computational cost comparisons would be valuable. The paper doesn't adequately explain why Meta-CP fails in Figure 3c (is this a fundamental limitation, tuning issue, or implementation problem?).
   - PS and PS-Test baselines are somewhat strawman comparisons that don't reflect the state-of-the-art in few-shot learning uncertainty quantification.
   - Parameter choices (δ = 10^-5, α = 0.1, ε = 0.1) appear arbitrary with no sensitivity analysis or justification.

7. **Missing technical analyses**:
   - No finite-sample complexity analysis is provided. The paper doesn't characterize how N, n, t must scale with δ, α, ε, which is crucial for practitioners.
   - The budget split (ε/2, α/2) in Algorithm 1 appears arbitrary. Is this optimal, or would other allocations improve prediction set size? An ablation study would clarify this design choice.
   - The guarantee depends critically on the quality of the meta-learned score function f, but the paper doesn't analyze how meta-learning error affects prediction set size or final performance.
   - No discussion of computational complexity or scalability analysis.

## Minor Weaknesses

8. **Limited novelty in technical approach**: While the contribution of being the second paper to use training-conditional conformal for meta-learning has some value, the extension itself is relatively straightforward. The paper conditions on both the calibration set and meta-learned parameters, which is a natural extension of existing results, though perhaps not previously published. This limits the technical novelty, though the work is still sound.

9. **Incomplete discussion of limitations and societal impact**: The authors acknowledge that users might misuse guarantees without understanding assumptions (Appendix F), but this deserves more prominent discussion in the main text, particularly given the healthcare applications. Potential harms include: (a) false sense of security from guarantees that may not hold under distribution shift, (b) conservative prediction sets in healthcare that delay diagnosis, and (c) autonomous driving applications where overly conservative sets reduce system utility. The paper should more thoroughly discuss failure modes and cases where the method might produce misleading guarantees.

## Questions for Authors

1. **
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
POST DISCUSSION:

The authors addressed most of my issues about readability and the attribution problem, so I have increased my score to a weak accept.

My final assessment is as follows:
I believe the proofs are correct, and the related work is correct.
I think the paper is the first to do a two-stage training-conditional guarantee, which has some value. Although this guarantee is a straightforward extension of existing results, perhaps somebody might be interested in it, and I do not believe it has appeared before.

I also believe it contributes by being the second paper to use training-conditional conformal for meta-learning. It is slightly different from the existing work by Adam Fisch et al. in that it also conditions on the tasks. Maybe somebody will find that useful!

_____________
This paper introduces a form of distribution-free uncertainty quantification guarantee into meta-learning and proposes an algorithm to achieve it. The guarantee is a triply-nested one, where the innermost integral is an expectation on a 0-1 loss. This expectation is nested within two other probabilities, conditioned on the calibration set and the meta learned parameters.  The algorithm boils down to two applications of binomial concentration. There are then extensive experiments on several meta learning tasks in vision, language, and medicine.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strengths: I the technical results are clearly correct. The experiments are comprehensive.

Weaknesses:

(1) The guarantee provided in this paper is so complicated to interpret that I worry it will never be used.  It is already difficult enough to interpret a tolerance interval, and adding an extra parameter incurs such a high cognitive cost for the user that one may never know how to set these parameters.

(2) The paper is difficult to understand, as the writing is dense and exceedingly complicated. For example, take Figure 2. It includes a lot of extraneous information, the colors are missing, and certain aspects of the plot are not explained. What do the orientations of the stripes mean, for example? What does the box with “N” mean? Another part that is hard to read is 4.1.  There is just too much going on and I’m not sure anybody except for a very small community working on these PAC type guarantees will understand it.

(3) The technical result is not too interesting and the domain area is not novel.  The meta conformal prediction paper already provides a guarantee in this problem domain that works well, although it is marginal on the meta learned parameters. The fact that we can apply binomial concentration twice as opposed to once in order to condition again on those variables is basically an extension of existing results. Having said that, I think it is interesting and clever that we can chain the concentration argument twice. Nice work on the algorithm. 

(4) I believe there is a major attribution issue throughout the work. The idea of training-conditional prediction sets was first proposed by Vovk [21]. Rebranding it as PAC prediction sets and citing both works as the source of the idea is wrong. The idea comes from Vovk and his paper should get all of the credit for that.  Please remove all instances of the following attribution: “PAC prediction sets [3]”. The same is true of the so-called PS-Binom algorithm, which is essentially the same as that of Vovk in Proposition 2b of [21].  The paper [3] can be credited for applying [21] in a deep learning setting, but [21] should be cited as the sole inventor of this idea, in this and any future work. I also take some issue with the rebranding as PAC prediction sets as opposed to training-conditional validity, as they are exactly equivalent and the latter terminology came first. I will argue for rejection of the paper unless these attributions are amended.

Questions:
What does it mean for tasks to be “iid” in the real world, outside of synthetic setups? What are some realistic settings where we might expect the guarantee provided from the authors to actually hold?

Limitations:
I believe the limitations have been addressed. (I do not believe there are any serious ethical limitations of this paper in the first place, and ethics review should certainly not get in the way of the publication of this paper.)

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

reviews.text:
# Hybrid Peer Review

## Summary

This paper proposes Meta-PS, an algorithm for constructing Probably Approximately Correct (PAC) prediction sets in the meta-learning setting. The key contribution is extending PAC prediction sets to handle three sources of randomness: calibration task distributions, test task adaptation sets, and test evaluation data. The authors introduce a meta-PAC (mPAC) guarantee (Definition 3) with parameters (ε, α, δ) that controls error with high probability over future tasks, which represents a meaningful extension beyond prior meta-conformal prediction approaches that only provide average-case guarantees across tasks. The algorithm operates in two steps: (1) constructing PAC prediction sets for each calibration task using adapted score functions, and (2) constructing a prediction interval over these parameters. The approach is evaluated on four datasets across three application domains (vision, NLP, healthcare) with competitive results demonstrating empirical satisfaction of the theoretical guarantees.

## Strengths

1. **Well-motivated theoretical contribution**: The paper addresses a genuine gap in meta-learning uncertainty quantification. The distinction between task-marginal guarantees (existing meta-conformal prediction) and task-conditional guarantees (this work) is important and well-articulated for safety-critical applications. The mPAC guarantee properly captures the hierarchical randomness sources in meta-learning, with Figure 2 effectively illustrating the conceptual difference from existing approaches through graphical models.

2. **Solid theoretical framework**: Theorem 1 is proven correctly, and the paper provides complete proofs while carefully stating assumptions. The three-parameter formulation (ε, α, δ) is rigorous and well-presented in Section 4.1, offering finer-grained control over randomness sources than prior work.

3. **Practical algorithm design**: Algorithm 1 is elegantly simple and modular, reusing existing PAC prediction set algorithms as subroutines (such as PS-BINOM), which makes the approach broadly applicable to any score function. The two-step design—meta calibration via PAC prediction sets on each task, then prediction interval construction over parameters—is intuitive and well-justified.

4. **Comprehensive experimental validation**: The evaluation spans multiple domains (mini-ImageNet and CIFAR10-C for vision, FewRel for NLP, CDC Heart Dataset for healthcare) with appropriate baselines and evaluation metrics. The empirical validation of the three-parameter guarantee is convincing, with proper statistical reporting using box plots and multiple trials (100 calibration trials × 50 test trials).

5. **Computational efficiency**: The method reuses the same parameter τ across adaptation without requiring additional calibration on test tasks, which is computationally efficient compared to approaches that would require per-task recalibration.

## Weaknesses

1. **Presentation and clarity issues**: The paper suffers from significant presentation problems that hinder accessibility. The notation is confusing and often misleading—particularly in Section 4.1 with multiple uses of f, f^A, f^{Ai} that could be simplified. More critically, a "big picture" is missing: while practitioners familiar with [1] may understand that f is a pre-trained meta-model, this context is absent from Figure 1 and the main exposition, which may confuse readers unfamiliar with the meta-learning literature. The paper is not self-contained; readers unfamiliar with Park et al. (2020) will struggle to understand the foundational concepts. We recommend the authors dedicate several paragraphs to explaining essential background before diving into technical details. Additionally, Algorithm 1 references Algorithm 2 (PS-BINOM) in the appendix without explaining what it does, making Algorithm 1 difficult to understand standalone.

2. **Limited algorithmic novelty**: While the extension to meta-learning is valuable, the algorithmic contribution is relatively straightforward. The core technique essentially applies existing PAC prediction set methods hierarchically—first to individual calibration tasks, then to the distribution of parameters. The proof strategy (Theorem 1) appears to be a natural application of existing PAC techniques to the hierarchical setting, with the key insight—that parameters are i.i.d. across tasks—being relatively intuitive given the problem structure.

3. **Insufficient experimental evaluation**:
   - **Single meta-learning algorithm**: Only prototypical networks are used as the score function. Robustness to other meta-learning algorithms (MAML, Relation Networks, etc.) remains unclear, and it is uncertain whether the guarantee holds independent of the meta-learning approach.
   - **Limited baseline comparisons**: Meta-CP is the only meta-learning baseline; comparison with other uncertainty quantification methods in meta-learning (Bayesian approaches, ensemble methods) would significantly strengthen the evaluation. The PS and PS-Test baselines serve somewhat as strawman comparisons.
   - **Missing ablation and sensitivity studies**: No ablation studies examine key design choices (e.g., why split δ equally as δ/2 in both steps of Algorithm 1?). Critical sensitivity analyses are absent: how do results vary with N (number of calibration tasks), n (calibration set size), and t (adaptation set size)? Only single configurations are tested per dataset.
   - **Unclear Meta-CP comparison**: Figure 3c shows Meta-CP fails to control error over test tasks, but the paper does not clearly explain whether this is a fundamental limitation, a tuning issue, or an implementation problem. Rerunning with optimized hyperparameters would clarify this.
   - **Fairness of comparisons**: PS-Test uses 20 shots from the test distribution while Meta-PS uses only 5 shots for adaptation, making the comparison somewhat unfair.

4. **Practical limitations under-explored**:
   - **Calibration data requirements**: The method requires N calibration tasks and n calibration examples per task. For mini-ImageNet experiments, N=500 and n=2500 is quite large. The paper acknowledges this limitation but provides no concrete guidance for practitioners (e.g., N ≥ f(ε, δ, |Y|)) or analysis of how performance degrades with insufficient data. No finite-sample complexity analysis is provided characterizing how N, n, t must scale with δ, α, ε.
   - **Exchangeability assumption**: The algorithm assumes calibration and test tasks are exchangeable samples from p_π, which may not hold in practice when tasks exhibit temporal or hierarchical structure (e.g., geographic regions in autonomous driving may have correlated weather patterns). The paper briefly mentions this in examples but doesn't explore robustness to task distribution mismatch or provide empirical validation under slight distribution shifts.
   - **Adaptation set sensitivity**: The method requires a separate adaptation set A for each task and reuses the same parameter τ after adaptation, assuming A is representative. For small t, this may fail. The paper does not thoroughly analyze sensitivity to adaptation set size or provide guidance on selecting t. For instance, the CDC Heart experiment uses only t=10 adaptation examples, which may be insufficient for reliable adaptation.
   - **Score function quality dependency**: The guarantee depends critically on the meta-learned score function f. Poor meta-learning leads to poor prediction sets, but the paper does not analyze how meta-learning quality affects the final guarantee or provide guidance on detecting when f is poorly trained.

5. **Missing computational and scalability analysis**:
   - No discussion of computational complexity or wall-clock time comparisons with Meta-CP.
   - The two-stage algorithm requires running PAC prediction set construction N times, which could be computationally expensive, but this cost is not analyzed.
   - No discussion of how prediction set size scales with the number of label classes or applicability to large-scale problems (e.g., ImageNet-scale scenarios with many classes).

6. **Insufficient discussion of when the guarantee matters**: While the paper motivates the need for task-conditional guarantees, experiments do not clearly demonstrate scenarios where Meta-CP's task-marginal guarantee would be problematic in practice beyond Figures 3c and 6. More detailed analysis would strengthen the case for why the stronger guarantee is necessary.

## Technical Questions and Concerns

1. **Algorithm design choices**: Why is the parameter δ split equally as δ/2 in both Algorithm 1 steps (Lines 3 and 5)? Is this allocation optimal, or would an unequal split (e.g., δ/3 and 2δ/3) improve prediction set size? Have the authors considered optimizing this split?

2. **Score function specifications**: The score functions in lines 108 and 106 have different input spaces. Can the authors explain why? The understanding is that f is a meta-learned off-the-shelf model, so the input specification differences require clarification. Additionally, why is the score function g defined as g(τ,y) = τ·𝟙(y=1) specifically? Have alternatives been considered?

3. **Estimator selection**: The authors select PS-Binom to estimate τ. Do different estimators make a significant difference in performance? An empirical comparison would be valuable.

4. **Evaluation metrics trade-off**: Both

title: Grounded Reinforcement Learning: Learning to Win the Game under Human Commands

reviews.summary:
Paper introduces a new task called “Grounded Reinforcement Learning” in which agents must be reward-maximizing with respect to some high-level language “constraint” (given by another agent/human). The authors demonstrate that existing vanilla RL, constrained RL (CRL), and behavior cloning (BC) fail to perform well both with and without commands in the MiniRTS environment (an example of this problem setup).

To solve this problem setting, the authors propose a new method RED - Reinforced Demonstration Distillation, which repeatedly alternates between pure RL and BC updates.  The authors demonstrate that RED is both the strongest and most grounded policy. The authors provide, through ablations, compelling arguments.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths:

Introduces (somewhat) of a new problem statement

Clearly written 

Human Evaluation is compelling

Impactful work from a safety perspective. 

Weaknesses:

My largest criticism is the use of the word "language" and i think this undermines the entire approach - the current representation treats commands as one-hot representations. These are not learnt, shaped or any different to the state space. This has no standard components which i would consider natural language, such as pragmatics, syntax and semantics.

RED algorithm does not seem novel (effectively mixture of two)

Results are not significantly better than baselines.
reviews.questions:
1) It is unclear how this approach is different from having standard RL with a combined state space over both command and game state.  As the command representation is not learnt - it seems unclear how this actually is different to a standard RL problem.

2) Can more justification be given to how difficult the opponent you play with is? Can you play with a harder agent to more clearly demonstrate the advantages of your method?

3) It is really unclear how the command token is meaningfully different to having an additional dimension within the game state. Can some justification be given to how either this representation is learnt or has any assemblance of language (is it compositional, have semantic or pragmatic properties).
reviews.limitations:
Addressing the above questions would be useful. 

I also believe actually using a language model would take great steps in demonstrating the effectiveness of your approach.
reviews.rating: 7: Accept: Technically solid paper, with high impact on at least one sub-area, or moderate-to-high impact on more than one areas, with good-to-excellent evaluation, resources, reproducibility, and no unaddressed ethical considerations.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.

To solve this problem setting, the authors propose a new method RED - Reinforced Demonstration Distillation, which repeatedly alternates between pure RL and BC updates.  The authors demonstrate that RED is both the strongest and most grounded policy. The authors provide, through ablations, compelling arguments.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strengths:

Introduces (somewhat) of a new problem statement

Clearly written 

Human Evaluation is compelling

Impactful work from a safety perspective. 

Weaknesses:

My largest criticism is the use of the word "language" and i think this undermines the entire approach - the current representation treats commands as one-hot representations. These are not learnt, shaped or any different to the state space. This has no standard components which i would consider natural language, such as pragmatics, syntax and semantics.

RED algorithm does not seem novel (effectively mixture of two)

Questions:
