title: Understanding Robust Learning through the Lens of Representation Similarities

reviews.summary:
The authors examine the effects of adversarial robustness training on representation. Specifically, they use CKA to compare robust vs non-robust networks, benign vs. adversarial inputs, and how these comparisons change over the course of learning. They find the following results:
- Robust representations are less specialized; distant layers are more similar in robust networks, and block structure is weaker
- Early layers in robust networks are largely unaffected by adversarial examples; representations are similar for benign vs. perturbed inputs
- Deeper layers overfit during robust learning
- Models trained to be robust to different threat models have similar representations
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths: The paper follows in a well-established tradition of using representational similarity analysis to understand neural network behavior and training interventions. The analyses are well-motivated and straightforward. The results are generally presented clearly and easy to understand. The results are interesting.

Weaknesses: The paper seems somewhat limited in scope: It primarily addresses adversarial (worst-case) robustness, which is only one type of robustness. I'm not totally convinced of the utility of this work; it could at the very least do a better job situating itself within existing robustness research. It's unclear if any of the experiments were run in replicate. The figures captions could be more informative and self-contained.

Overall, I think this work could be suitable for publication if it is sufficiently revised.
reviews.questions:
This work primarily examines adversarial (i.e. worst-case) robustness, which is one of many types of robustness, among others including average-case robustness (Hendrycks and Dietterich, Benchmarking Neural Network Robustness to Common Corruptions and Perturbations) and natural adversarial examples (Hendrycks et al.). It’s not clear how important of a problem adversarial robustness is (see Gilmer et al.’s Motivating the Rules of the Game for Adversarial Example Research). The authors examine robustness to Gabor, snow, and jpeg attacks, but the analysis and results are limited. I would encourage the researchers to more systematically examine how their findings extend to other types of robustness.

Are the results from a single instance of each model? If so, I would strongly encourage the authors to repeat their analyses in triplicate at a minimum. If this is computationally cost-prohibitive, then perhaps they could focus on the most important results.

There is relevant previous work examining the relationship between representational dimensionality and adversarial robustness (Leavitt and Morcos, Linking average- and worst-case perturbation robustness via class selectivity and dimensionality; Sanyal et al., Robustness vai Deep Low-rank Representations; Nayebi and Ganguli, Biologically inspired protection of deep networks from adversarial attacks). These papers show that low rank representations confer adversarial robustness. Can you reconcile these results with your findings that adversarial robustness is associated with greater utilization of model capacity? Additionally, most of these works (certainly Leavitt and Morcos and Sanyal et al.) regularized representational dimensionality and found that it improved robustness; it would be quite interesting to investigate whether that the inverse holds: does adversarial training reduce representational dimensionality?

The claim that “Deeper layers overfit during robust learning” seems like it could be leveraged to improve the generalization gap caused by robust learning. The simplest approach would be to simply stop training when the adversarial loss begins to rise (as in Figure 6). I assume this has drawbacks, but the authors don’t present the data (e.g. accuracy curves over training). Is this overfitting a necessary component of robust learning?

Lines 59-61: “On the other hand, the representations of benign and perturbed inputs from robust networks are indistinguishable from one another with regards to representation similarity metrics.” I think to make this claim the authors need to directly compare CKA(Benign, Perturbed) to CKA(Benign, Benign) and CKA(Perturbed, Perturbed).

The caption for Figure 2 should describe the model architecture(s) used to generate the results, as well as whether the data are benign or perturbed. There are other Figures which lack important experimental details, such as whether the data are benign or perturbed.

Comparing the CKA results in Figure 2d to the results in Figures 2a-c make it clear that the similarity between robust and non-robust networks is lower than the similarity between networks of the same type, but I think it’s important to have a negative baseline—how low is “low”? Accordingly, I think the authors should repeat the analysis (CKA between robust and non-robust networks) using sample-shuffled data (and/or some other suitable random baseline).

Showing that the block structure effect varies with the strength of adversarial training is a nice experiment and result.

The results presented in lines 174-188 (“Impact of robust training strength” and “Impact of architecture”) would be easier to interpret (and their motivation clearer) if you introduced them with Nguyen et al.’s finding that “block structure in the internal representations arises in models that are heavily overparameterized relative to the training dataset.” While you do cite their work (“Previously Nguyen et al. [28] observed that increasing network width, thus capacity, leads to emergence of block-structure in non-robust networks”), I think this is an insufficient description of their results and should be presented earlier.

“Overfitting is predominantly visible in later layers”: I would suggest a follow-up experiment to more thoroughly test this claim: freeze early layers (blocks 1 and/or 2) early in training. If early layers aren’t involved in the overfitting and training instability, then the freezing should have minimal effect on these phenomena.

Lines 278-280: “When using CKA to compare against the Snow threat model, we observe that the highest average similarity is achieved with Gabor. This represents a novel insight into these threat classes, as correspondence between the two was not previously known.” This result seems very specific and not particularly useful. I would encourage the authors to find a more general result (see my earlier comment about examining different attack types).

minor comments:

Lines 21-22: “...such as images, speech or text”. It’s a matter of personal taste, but I am a proponent of the Oxford comma: “...such as images, speech, or text”

Line 22: What does “meaningful” mean in this context? Meaningful with regards to what?

Line 187: “These results suggest that while increasing width in robust networks doesn’t lead a drastic shift in similarity of internal layer representations.” Typo?

Typo in caption of Figure 5 (...to understand similairty)
reviews.limitations: The authors devote a paragraph at the end of the discussion to the limitations of their study, which seems appropriate given the space limitations. I do, however, think the authors could be more careful and nuanced about some of their claims.
reviews.rating: 7: Accept: Technically solid paper, with high impact on at least one sub-area, or moderate-to-high impact on more than one areas, with good-to-excellent evaluation, resources, reproducibility, and no unaddressed ethical considerations.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.

[28] observed that increasing network width, thus capacity, leads to emergence of block-structure in non-robust networks”), I think this is an insufficient description of their results and should be presented earlier.

“Overfitting is predominantly visible in later layers”: I would suggest a follow-up experiment to more thoroughly test this claim: freeze early layers (blocks 1 and/or 2) early in training. If early layers aren’t involved in the overfitting and training instability, then the freezing should have minimal effect on these phenomena.

I would encourage the authors to find a more general result (see my earlier comment about examining different attack types).

minor comments:

Lines 21-22: “...such as images, speech or text”. It’s a matter of personal taste, but I am a proponent of the Oxford comma: “...such as images, speech, or text”

Line 22: What does “meaningful” mean in this context? Meaningful with regards to what?

Line 187: “These results suggest that while increasing width in robust networks doesn’t lead a drastic shift in similarity of internal layer representations.” Typo?

Typo in caption of Figure 5 (...to understand similairty)

7: Accept: Technically solid paper, with high impact on at least one sub-area, or moderate-to-high impact on more than one areas, with good-to-excellent evaluation, resources, reproducibility, and no unaddressed ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
# Hybrid Peer Review

## Summary

This paper investigates how representations learned by adversarially robust neural networks differ from those learned by standard non-robust networks. Using Centered Kernel Alignment (CKA) as the primary representation similarity metric, the authors conduct a comprehensive empirical study across CIFAR-10, ImageNette, and ImageWoof datasets with multiple architectures. The key findings are: (1) robust networks exhibit higher cross-layer similarity and lack the characteristic 'block structure' observed in non-robust networks; (2) early layers remain largely unaffected by adversarial perturbations even in non-robust networks; (3) deeper layers exhibit overfitting during robust training while early layers converge quickly; and (4) representations from visually dissimilar threat models can show surprising similarity. The authors position these observations as diagnostic insights to guide future improvements in robust network design and training.

## Strengths

The paper addresses an important and timely question about understanding why robust networks suffer from degraded benign accuracy and poor generalization. It follows in a well-established tradition of using representational similarity analysis to understand neural network behavior and training interventions. The use of CKA for layer-wise representation analysis is appropriate and well-motivated, with clear justification for computational efficiency and theoretical properties over alternative metrics.

The experimental scope is commendable, systematically varying multiple factors including network width and depth, adversarial budget (ε), threat models, and datasets. The findings demonstrate consistency across these variations, suggesting robustness of the observations. The work is technically sound, with comprehensive ablations across the appendices and clear presentation of main findings through effective visualizations that communicate complex high-dimensional comparisons. The authors acknowledge limitations honestly and provide open-sourced code to support reproducibility.

The paper provides interpretable layer-wise insights that aggregate metrics like accuracy cannot offer. Particularly valuable are the observations about layer-specific convergence patterns during robust training and the surprising finding that visually dissimilar threat models can produce similar robust representations. The discussion section also proposes concrete directions grounded in observations, such as layer freezing and layer-wise regularization strategies.

## Weaknesses and Limitations

Despite these strengths, the paper has notable limitations that should be addressed. Most significantly, the work is primarily observational and correlational rather than causal. While the authors acknowledge this limitation, it substantially restricts the actionability of their findings. The paper identifies interesting patterns—such as lack of specialization in robust networks and layer-wise overfitting—but does not rigorously establish why these phenomena occur or prove they directly cause the observed performance degradation. For instance, is the lack of specialization a root cause of poor benign accuracy, or merely a symptom of other optimization dynamics? This distinction is crucial for understanding whether the proposed interventions would actually improve robust learning.

The reliance on CKA as the sole primary metric is concerning. Although the authors mention similar insights from other metrics (SVCCA, Procrustes) in Appendix B.1, this comparison is insufficiently detailed. CKA has known limitations—the authors themselves note its insensitivity to principal component deletions—yet do not thoroughly validate that these limitations do not affect their conclusions. A more rigorous validation across multiple complementary metrics would substantially strengthen confidence in the findings.

The paper provides limited theoretical justification for why robust training should lead to the observed representation properties. The hypothesis about reduced relative capacity is intriguing but underdeveloped. Can this claim be formalized? Does increasing ε by a factor k produce the same effect on representation similarity as reducing width by a factor k? Quantitative analysis would strengthen this central claim.

The proposed improvements (layer freezing, layer-wise regularization, threat model selection) are speculative and lack empirical validation within the paper itself. For the claim that "deeper layers overfit during robust learning," while this could potentially be leveraged to improve the generalization gap, the authors do not present the data to support this—for example, accuracy curves over training are absent. The simplest approach would be to stop training when adversarial loss begins to rise (as suggested by Figure 6), but is such overfitting a necessary component of robust learning? This remains unclear.

Regarding the scope of the work, the paper focuses exclusively on adversarial (worst-case) robustness, which is only one type of robustness among others including average-case robustness (e.g., Hendrycks and Dietterich's common corruptions) and natural adversarial examples. While the authors examine robustness to Gabor, snow, and jpeg attacks, this analysis remains limited. The paper does not systematically examine how findings extend to other types of robustness, nor does it adequately situate itself within the broader robustness research landscape. Additionally, it is unclear how important adversarial robustness is as a research problem (see Gilmer et al.'s work on motivating adversarial example research), and the paper would benefit from more explicit positioning on this question.

The experiments are restricted to image classification on relatively small datasets (CIFAR-10, ImageNette/Woof subsets of ImageNet) due to computational constraints. Full ImageNet results would substantially strengthen claims about scalability. Furthermore, all experiments use primarily ResNets and Wide-ResNets. Modern architectures such as Vision Transformers are mentioned only briefly, yet their fundamentally different inductive biases may lead to different representation properties. The generalization to other domains (NLP, audio, graphs) is entirely unexplored, and the paper should explicitly acknowledge this scope limitation.

Regarding reproducibility, while code is promised, there is uncertainty about whether experiments were run in replicate. The paper does not clearly specify if results represent single model instances or multiple runs with confidence intervals. If results are from single instances, this is a significant concern—the authors should repeat analyses in triplicate at minimum, or if computationally prohibitive, focus on the most important results. Additionally, some experimental details lack clarity. For instance, the caption for Figure 2 should describe the model architecture(s) used and whether data are benign or perturbed. Multiple figures lack such important details, which hampers reproducibility.

## Specific Technical Concerns

Several specific technical issues warrant attention:

**Statistical validation and baselines:** To claim that "representations of benign and perturbed inputs from robust networks are indistinguishable," the authors should directly compare CKA(Benign, Perturbed) to CKA(Benign, Benign) and CKA(Perturbed, Perturbed). Additionally, when comparing CKA results between robust and non-robust networks (Figure 2d versus Figures 2a-c), the paper should include negative baselines—how low is "low"? Repeating this analysis with sample-shuffled data would provide necessary context for interpreting similarity values.

**Block structure and capacity:** The results on block structure variation with adversarial training strength (Section 3) are valuable, but the motivation would be clearer if introduced with Nguyen et al.'s finding that block structure arises in heavily overparameterized models. While this work is cited, it deserves earlier and more thorough presentation. The claim that "overfitting is predominantly visible in later layers" would benefit from follow-up experiments such as freezing early layers (blocks 1 and/or 2) during training to test whether this has minimal effect on overfitting phenomena.

**Threat model analysis:** The observation that Snow and Gabor threat models show the highest average similarity (Section 6) is intriguing but lacks mechanistic explanation. Why do visually dissimilar attacks produce similar representations? This finding seems specific rather than providing general insights, and the paper would benefit from identifying more broadly applicable patterns across threat models.

**Reconciliation with prior work:** The paper does not adequately reconcile its findings with related work on representational dimensionality and robustness. Prior work (Leavitt and Morcos, Sanyal et al.) shows that low-rank representations confer adversarial robustness, while the present findings suggest adversarial robustness is associated with greater utilization of model capacity. How do these observations fit together? Does adversarial training actually reduce representational dimensionality, and if so, does this contradict the paper's claims about reduced specialization?

## Minor Issues

The paper contains several minor issues: (1) Line 21-22 would benefit from the Oxford comma in "images, speech, or text"; (2) Line 22's use of "meaningful" needs clarification; (3) Line 187 appears to contain a typo; (4) The caption of Figure 5 contains "similairty" instead of "similarity." More substantially, figure captions should be more informative and self-contained, providing experimental details without requiring readers to consult the main text.

## Questions for Authors

1. Can you provide causal evidence that the observed lack of specialization in robust networks directly causes reduced benign accuracy? Have you tested interventions (e.g., regularization promoting layer differentiation) to validate this hypothesis?

2. How sensitive are your findings to the choice of CKA? Can you validate key findings with other representation similarity metrics (SVCCA, Procrustes, PWCCA) beyond the brief
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
The authors examine the effects of adversarial robustness training on representation. Specifically, they use CKA to compare robust vs non-robust networks, benign vs. adversarial inputs, and how these comparisons change over the course of learning. They find the following results:
- Robust representations are less specialized; distant layers are more similar in robust networks, and block structure is weaker
- Early layers in robust networks are largely unaffected by adversarial examples; representations are similar for benign vs. perturbed inputs
- Deeper layers overfit during robust learning
- Models trained to be robust to different threat models have similar representations

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strengths: The paper follows in a well-established tradition of using representational similarity analysis to understand neural network behavior and training interventions. The analyses are well-motivated and straightforward. The results are generally presented clearly and easy to understand. The results are interesting.

Weaknesses: The paper seems somewhat limited in scope: It primarily addresses adversarial (worst-case) robustness, which is only one type of robustness. I'm not totally convinced of the utility of this work; it could at the very least do a better job situating itself within existing robustness research. It's unclear if any of the experiments were run in replicate. The figures captions could be more informative and self-contained.

Overall, I think this work could be suitable for publication if it is sufficiently revised.

Questions:
This work primarily examines adversarial (i.e. worst-case) robustness, which is one of many types of robustness, among others including average-case robustness (Hendrycks and Dietterich, Benchmarking Neural Network Robustness to Common Corruptions and Perturbations) and natural adversarial examples (Hendrycks et al.). It’s not clear how important of a problem adversarial robustness is (see Gilmer et al.’s Motivating the Rules of the Game for Adversarial Example Research). The authors examine robustness to Gabor, snow, and jpeg attacks, but the analysis and results are limited. I would encourage the researchers to more systematically examine how their findings extend to other types of robustness.

Are the results from a single instance of each model? If so, I would strongly encourage the authors to repeat their analyses in triplicate at a minimum. If this is computationally cost-prohibitive, then perhaps they could focus on the most important results.

There is relevant previous work examining the relationship between representational dimensionality and adversarial robustness (Leavitt and Morcos, Linking average- and worst-case perturbation robustness via class selectivity and dimensionality; Sanyal et al., Robustness vai Deep Low-rank Representations; Nayebi and Ganguli, Biologically inspired protection of deep networks from adversarial attacks). These papers show that low rank representations confer adversarial robustness. Can you reconcile these results with your findings that adversarial robustness is associated with greater utilization of model capacity? Additionally, most of these works (certainly Leavitt and Morcos and Sanyal et al.) regularized representational dimensionality and found that it improved robustness; it would be quite interesting to investigate whether that the inverse holds: does adversarial training reduce representational dimensionality?

The claim that “Deeper layers overfit during robust learning” seems like it could be leveraged to improve the generalization gap caused by robust learning. The simplest approach would be to simply stop training when the adversarial loss begins to rise (as in Figure 6). I assume this has drawbacks, but the authors don’t present the data (e.g. accuracy curves over training). Is this overfitting a necessary component of robust learning?

Lines 59-61: “On the other hand, the representations of benign and perturbed inputs from robust networks are indistinguishable from one another with regards to representation similarity metrics.” I think to make this claim the authors need to directly compare CKA(Benign, Perturbed) to CKA(Benign, Benign) and CKA(Perturbed, Perturbed).

The caption for Figure 2 should describe the model architecture(s) used to generate the results, as well as whether the data are benign or perturbed. There are other Figures which lack important experimental details, such as whether the data are benign or perturbed.

Comparing the CKA results in Figure 2d to the results in Figures 2a-c make it clear that the similarity between robust and non-robust networks is lower than the similarity between networks of the same type, but I think it’s important to have a negative baseline—how low is “low”? Accordingly, I think the authors should repeat the analysis (CKA between robust and non-robust networks) using sample-shuffled data (and/or some other suitable random baseline).

Showing that the block structure effect varies with the strength of adversarial training is a nice experiment and result.

The results presented in lines 174-188 (“Impact of robust training strength” and “Impact of architecture”) would be easier to interpret (and their motivation clearer) if you introduced them with Nguyen et al.’s finding that “block structure in the internal representations arises in models that are heavily overparameterized relative to the training dataset.” While you do cite their work (“Previously Nguyen et al. [28] observed that increasing network width, thus capacity, leads to emergence of block-structure in non-robust networks”), I think this is an insufficient description of their results and should be presented earlier.

“Overfitting is predominantly visible in later layers”: I would suggest a follow-up experiment to more thoroughly test this claim: freeze early layers (blocks 1 and/or 2) early in training. If early layers aren’t involved in the overfitting and training instability, then the freezing should have minimal effect on these phenomena.

Lines 278-280: “When using CKA to compare against the Snow threat model, we observe that the highest average similarity is achieved with Gabor. This represents a novel insight into these threat classes, as correspondence between the two was not previously known.” This result seems very specific and not particularly useful. I would encourage the authors to find a more general result (see my earlier comment about examining different attack types).

minor comments:

Lines 21-22: “...such as images, speech or text”. It’s a matter of personal taste, but I am a proponent of the Oxford comma: “...such as images, speech, or text”

Line 22: What does “meaningful” mean in this context? Meaningful with regards to what?

Line 187: “These results suggest that while increasing width in robust networks doesn’t lead a drastic shift in similarity of internal layer representations.” Typo?

Typo in caption of Figure 5 (...to understand similairty)

Limitations:
The authors devote a paragraph at the end of the discussion to the limitations of their study, which seems appropriate given the space limitations. I do, however, think the authors could be more careful and nuanced about some of their claims.

Rating:
7: Accept: Technically solid paper, with high impact on at least one sub-area, or moderate-to-high impact on more than one areas, with good-to-excellent evaluation, resources, reproducibility, and no unaddressed ethical considerations.

reviews.summary: This paper uses an existing method for comparisons of intermediate neural network activations (CKA) for a comparison of robust and non-robust networks. The authors analyze the similarities in different aspects and try to deduce insights in adversarial training.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
- The paper uses an interesting & potentially insightful approach to better understand how adversarially trained networks process information and how they differ from non-robust networks.
- The attached code for this submission is well documented and looks clean, making the results more trustworthy.
- It feels like the paper spends too much time describing plots/results compared to interpreting the results and presenting hypotheses for what results mean.
reviews.questions:
- Missing related work: There has been previous work on comparing the features of robust vs. non-robust neural networks that should be cited, e.g. using feature visualizations [1].
- L25: Reference missing.
- L45f: Reference [18] should be placed behind Imagewoof and not behind Imagenet; also, a reference for ImageNet is missing. 
- L93: E.g., JPEG compression doesn't seem like an additive perturbation.
- L108: If this is well known, I encourage the authors to support this sentence with (multiple) references.
- L118: A more detailed critical discussion of the possible shortcomings of these metrics and why they don't impact the results of this paper would be good.
- L123f: Why doesn't this impact your results? This definitely needs to be supported by a strong argument. 
- Figure 2/3: On which datasets were these metrics calculated? On the vanilla test data or on adversarially perturbed versions of the test data? What does the figure look like for the other data type (either benign or adversarial)?
- Figure 2/3: Given that the clean accuracy goes down for adversarial training, I'm wondering whether the change in the similarity plots is really due to a special aspect of adversarial training or just due to the drop in performance, i.e. how do these plots look like for non-robust networks that are not trained until convergence but until they reach a similar test accuracy as the robust networks?
- L163f: That seems a bit like an overstatement: The block structure is still clearly visible for 2 out of the 3 datasets - it just gets weaker.
- Multiple typos/grammar mistakes that make it sometimes break the flow of the text, e.g. L172, L187, L201, L216, L221, L235.
- Figure 4: These plots should be larger - the text is hard to read.
- Figure 4: The first sentence of the caption doesn't read right/is difficult to parse.
- L185f: Did the authors observe the same behavior as described by Nguyen et al. when they tried to reproduce their observations for non-robust networks? At the moment it is difficult to confidently say that the observation reported here is because of the robust network or because of the experimental setup used by the authors.
- L213: If this is well known, I again encourage the authors to support this sentence with (multiple) references. Furthermore, this is actually not such a clear property, and there are specific attacks that just aim to create adversarial examples that transfer well between different models.
- Minor comment: The paper uses inconsistent notation - sometimes the authors say "Figure", sometimes "figure"; sometimes "Appendix", sometimes "App.".
- L252f: Where can we see both validation and training loss? The figure only shows one "loss" but doesn't say which one it is.
- L265: I wouldn't call these "adversarial perturbations" but rather use the more commonly used expression common corruptions. Especially, since at least for JPEG compression there is nothing you can optimize - so that doesn't really fit the overall adversarial framework.
- Section 6: What are the specific parameters of the JPEG, snow, and Gabor corruptions?
- L289: What is the conclusion/interpretation of the results?

[1] Leveraging Sparse Linear Layers for Debuggable Deep Networks. Eric Wong, Shibani Santurkar, Aleksander Mądry. 2021
reviews.limitations:
- While the authors mentioned criticism on the similarity metric they use (CKA), they don't really explain why this doesn't apply to their analysis. This should be properly addressed.
- It is also unclear whether the results differ if a different adversarial attack (with the same thread model) was used for generating the adversarial perturbations - this should ideally also be addressed.
reviews.rating: 6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

reviews.text:
# Hybrid Peer Review

## Summary

This paper investigates how representations learned by adversarially robust neural networks differ from those learned by standard non-robust networks. Using Centered Kernel Alignment (CKA) as the primary representation similarity metric, the authors conduct a comprehensive empirical study across CIFAR-10, ImageNette, and ImageWoof datasets with multiple architectures. Key findings include: (1) robust networks exhibit significantly higher cross-layer similarity and lack the characteristic 'block structure' observed in non-robust networks; (2) early layers remain largely unaffected by adversarial perturbations even in non-robust networks; (3) deeper layers exhibit overfitting during robust training while early layers converge quickly; and (4) visually dissimilar threat models can produce similar robust representations. The authors propose these observations as diagnostic insights to guide future improvements in robust network design and training.

## Strengths

The paper addresses an important and timely problem—understanding why robust networks suffer from degraded benign accuracy and poor generalization—through a novel lens of representation similarity analysis. This approach is potentially insightful for better understanding how adversarially trained networks process information differently from non-robust networks.

The use of CKA for layer-wise representation analysis is well-motivated and appropriately justified. The authors provide clear computational efficiency comparisons and theoretical justification for this choice over alternative metrics. The experimental scope is commendable, covering multiple datasets, architectures, threat models, and training methods with systematic ablations. The findings demonstrate consistency across these varied conditions, suggesting robustness of the observations.

The paper's presentation is generally clear and effective, with informative visualizations that successfully communicate complex high-dimensional comparisons. The attached code is well-documented and clean, making the results more trustworthy and supporting reproducibility. The authors honestly acknowledge limitations and demonstrate technical soundness throughout.

## Major Weaknesses

The paper is primarily observational and correlational rather than causal. While the authors acknowledge this limitation, it significantly constrains the actionability and impact of the work. The authors identify interesting patterns—such as lack of specialization in robust networks and layer-wise overfitting—but do not establish why these phenomena occur or rigorously prove they directly cause the observed performance degradation. For instance, is the lack of specialization a root cause of poor benign accuracy, or does it arise from other optimization dynamics? This distinction is critical for interpreting the findings.

The reliance on CKA as the sole primary metric is a notable concern. Although the paper mentions similar insights from SVCCA and Procrustes in Appendix B.1, the main analysis depends heavily on a single metric, which could miss important aspects of representation differences or introduce metric-specific artifacts. A more thorough validation across multiple complementary similarity metrics would significantly strengthen confidence in the robustness of the observations. The authors should provide quantitative comparisons showing that conclusions hold across all three metrics, particularly for key claims about block structure and cross-layer similarity.

The paper does not provide actionable improvements to robust training; it remains purely diagnostic. The proposed future directions (layer freezing, layer-wise regularization, threat model selection based on representation similarity) are speculative and lack empirical validation within the paper. Critically, the authors do not validate whether enforcing layer-wise differentiation through regularization would actually improve benign accuracy, or whether layer-freezing strategies grounded in their convergence observations would improve training efficiency or final robustness. This significantly limits the practical impact of the work.

## Moderate Weaknesses

The paper spends considerable time describing and presenting plots and results but comparatively less time interpreting what these results mean and developing mechanistic hypotheses. For example, the observation that increasing adversarial budget ε reduces block structure is interesting, but the explanation—that this reflects "relative capacity reduction"—is underdeveloped and lacks rigorous justification. Why should increased task complexity necessarily manifest as higher cross-layer similarity rather than other representation changes? Formalizing this claim or providing empirical evidence (e.g., measuring effective dimensionality of representations) would strengthen the analysis considerably.

The experimental scope, while broad in some dimensions, is limited in others. Experiments use relatively small datasets (CIFAR-10, ImageNette/ImageWoof subsets of ImageNet) rather than full ImageNet due to computational costs. This raises questions about whether findings scale to larger datasets and modern large-scale settings. Similarly, experiments focus primarily on ResNets and Wide-ResNets. Modern architectures such as Vision Transformers—which have fundamentally different inductive biases than CNNs—are not thoroughly analyzed. It remains unclear whether findings generalize to these increasingly important architectures.

The connection between observed representation properties and actual performance outcomes remains somewhat loose. While the paper links representation properties to performance degradation (e.g., benign accuracy loss), the quantitative relationships are not rigorously established. How much does lack of specialization contribute to the benign accuracy drop? Does the magnitude of cross-layer similarity differences correlate with the magnitude of performance degradation? The paper would benefit from more rigorous statistical analysis, including confidence intervals on CKA values and statistical tests of observed differences.

Several specific findings warrant deeper investigation. The observation that early layers are largely unaffected by adversarial perturbations even in non-robust networks is interesting, but the paper does not adequately explain this phenomenon or explore its implications. The surprising similarity between visually dissimilar threat models (Snow and Gabor) deserves more mechanistic explanation—can you provide intuition grounded in frequency analysis or other signal properties? Has this similarity been validated to extend to other visually dissimilar pairs?

## Minor Weaknesses and Technical Issues

The paper acknowledges criticisms of CKA as a similarity metric but does not thoroughly explain why these limitations do not impact the analysis. A more detailed critical discussion of CKA's known limitations (e.g., insensitivity to principal component deletions) and why they don't invalidate the paper's conclusions would be valuable. Additionally, it is unclear whether results differ if a different adversarial attack with the same threat model was used for generating adversarial perturbations—this should ideally be addressed.

Several presentation issues impede clarity. The paper contains multiple typos and grammatical mistakes (e.g., L172, L187, L201, L216, L221, L235) that break the flow of text. Notation is sometimes inconsistent—"Figure" vs. "figure," "Appendix" vs. "App." Figure 4 should be enlarged as the text is difficult to read, and the first sentence of its caption does not read clearly. Inconsistent terminology is also problematic: the paper uses "adversarial perturbations" to describe common corruptions like JPEG compression, which is misleading since JPEG compression cannot be optimized and doesn't fit the adversarial framework. The more standard expression "common corruptions" would be more appropriate.

Specific technical details require clarification. On which datasets were the metrics in Figures 2 and 3 calculated—on vanilla test data or adversarially perturbed versions? What do these figures look like for the other data type? Given that clean accuracy drops significantly for adversarial training, it is unclear whether changes in similarity plots reflect a special aspect of adversarial training or merely the performance drop itself. This should be addressed by comparing plots for non-robust networks trained only until they reach similar test accuracy as robust networks. Similarly, the claim that block structure disappears (L163f) appears somewhat overstated—the block structure is still clearly visible for 2 out of 3 datasets, merely weakened.

Missing and imprecise references are scattered throughout. Reference [1] is missing at L25. Reference [18] should be placed behind Imagewoof rather than ImageNet, and a reference for ImageNet itself is missing. L93 incorrectly categorizes JPEG compression as an additive perturbation. Claims stated as "well known" (L108, L213) should be supported by multiple citations. L118 needs a more detailed critical discussion of CKA's shortcomings and why they don't impact results. L123f requires strong argumentation for why known limitations of CKA don't affect the conclusions.

The paper also lacks important related work. Previous work on comparing features of robust vs. non-robust neural networks using feature visualizations should be cited and discussed. The transferability analysis (Section 4.2) references Nguyen et al., but the authors should clarify whether they reproduced those observations for non-robust networks—without this, it is difficult to confidently attribute findings to robust training rather than experimental setup.

Additional technical concerns include: What are the specific parameters of the JPEG, snow, and Gabor corruptions used in Section 6? Where can both validation and training loss be seen in the referenced figures—the paper mentions "loss" but doesn't specify which? When comparing different threat models (Section 6), how were perturbation budgets aligned across visually different attacks? Did the authors use perceptual metrics (e.g., LPIPS) to equate visual similarity? What is the specific conclusion or interpretation of the results in Section L289?

## Limitations and Scope

The authors acknowledge several key limitations: (1) all discoveries depend on the effectiveness of CKA as a representation similarity metric; (2) the study

reviews.original_rating: 6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

reviews.summary: The paper contrasts representation similarities of networks trained to perform image classification with and without adversarial noise. To do so, the authors measure the similarity of representations using the Centered Kernel Alignment (CKA) metric (as well two other similarity metrics in appendix) for CIFAR-10 and two subsets of ImageNet. The authors highlight 1) networks trained with adversarial noise have layers similar to one another compared to those of standard trained networks (which have a block structure) 2) representations in early layers are unaffected by adversarial perturbations both for standard and adversarially trained networks 3) networks trained with and without adversarial perturbations have similar representations until the last 10 or so layers 4) early layers converge faster and later layers overfit to local minima. The authors also analyze the similarity of representations when other perturbations are applied JPEG, Gabor, and Snow, finding
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
This work investigates representations learned for image classification by contrasting the representation similarities of networks trained with and without adversarial noise—leading to several insights into properties of the learned representations as well as learning dynamics of modern networks. Such properties as the authors point out are not captured by aggregate performance metrics such as loss or accuracy, leading to insights about the learned representations. The paper is well-written, experiments clearly described, and motivation is well-grounded.

The experiments conducted are convincing and well-formulated. For example, multiple similarity metrics are compared, variants of the adversarial perturbations are explored, and experimental claims are well-founded. 
However, the authors'claims should be sufficiently couched within the experimental settings studied: supervised image classification for ResNet-based models on CIFAR-10 and two subsets of ImageNet. For example, the work only studies ResNet-based architectures yet claims to cover “DNNs with different architectures” (line 12) and the “impact of choice of architecture” (line 37). I would expect a comparison of “different architectures” to encompass for example transformer-based architectures, MLP-architectures, etc. I suggest the authors more explicitly couch claims in the introduction, abstract, title, and conclusions within the confines of the experimental settings studied. For example 
The comparison of representations for "threat models" (JPEG, Gabor, and Snow) in Section 6 was not particularly informative and seemed removed from the primary findings of the remainder of the paper. 

Robust representations is a broad term. This work studies robustness to random noise in the input. Yet, the authors intermix “robustness to adversarial examples” and often plainly use the term “robust network” to describe a particular type: robustness to noise (defined via L-p bounds on the input) throughout the work. For example, robust in the context of image classification can just as well refer to robustness with respect to rendering method ImageNet-Sketch, artifacts such as blurring (ImageNet-C), adversarial examples (ImageNet-A), or even robustness to natural transformations such as pose (Alcorn et al.). I suggest the authors clarify the wording to only include the specific definitions of robustness studied here.

The insights gained from the authors’ analysis are interesting and well-described. The finding of most value, in my opinion, is that later layers overfit (matching existing work relating overfitting in later layers to spurious correlations [1]). While the analysis sheds light on differences between networks trained with and without adversarial noise as well as their learning dynamics the findings are confined to the L-infinity definition of robustness studied for supervised image classification using ResNet-based architectures (for the main claims in section 4 and 5).

[1] “Last Layer Re-Training is Sufficient for Robustness to Spurious Correlations” [https://arxiv.org/abs/2204.02937](https://arxiv.org/abs/2204.02937)
reviews.questions: included above

Summary:
The paper contrasts representation similarities of networks trained to perform image classification with and without adversarial noise. To do so, the authors measure the similarity of representations using the Centered Kernel Alignment (CKA) metric (as well two other similarity metrics in appendix) for CIFAR-10 and two subsets of ImageNet. The authors highlight 1) networks trained with adversarial noise have layers similar to one another compared to those of standard trained networks (which have a block structure) 2) representations in early layers are unaffected by adversarial perturbations both for standard and adversarially trained networks 3) networks trained with and without adversarial perturbations have similar representations until the last 10 or so layers 4) early layers converge faster and later layers overfit to local minima. The authors also analyze the similarity of representations when other perturbations are applied JPEG, Gabor, and Snow, finding

Strengths And Weaknesses:
This work investigates representations learned for image classification by contrasting the representation similarities of networks trained with and without adversarial noise—leading to several insights into properties of the learned representations as well as learning dynamics of modern networks. Such properties as the authors point out are not captured by aggregate performance metrics such as loss or accuracy, leading to insights about the learned representations. The paper is well-written, experiments clearly described, and motivation is well-grounded.

The experiments conducted are convincing and well-formulated. For example, multiple similarity metrics are compared, variants of the adversarial perturbations are explored, and experimental claims are well-founded. 
However, the authors'claims should be sufficiently couched within the experimental settings studied: supervised image classification for ResNet-based models on CIFAR-10 and two subsets of ImageNet. For example, the work only studies ResNet-based architectures yet claims to cover “DNNs with different architectures” (line 12) and the “impact of choice of architecture” (line 37). I would expect a comparison of “different architectures” to encompass for example transformer-based architectures, MLP-architectures, etc. I suggest the authors more explicitly couch claims in the introduction, abstract, title, and conclusions within the confines of the experimental settings studied. For example 
The comparison of representations for "threat models" (JPEG, Gabor, and Snow) in Section 6 was not particularly informative and seemed removed from the primary findings of the remainder of the paper. 

Robust representations is a broad term. This work studies robustness to random noise in the input. Yet, the authors intermix “robustness to adversarial examples” and often plainly use the term “robust network” to describe a particular type: robustness to noise (defined via L-p bounds on the input) throughout the work. For example, robust in the context of image classification can just as well refer to robustness with respect to rendering method ImageNet-Sketch, artifacts such as blurring (ImageNet-C), adversarial examples (ImageNet-A), or even robustness to natural transformations such as pose (Alcorn et al.). I suggest the authors clarify the wording to only include the specific definitions of robustness studied here.

The insights gained from the authors’ analysis are interesting and well-described. The finding of most value, in my opinion, is that later layers overfit (matching existing work relating overfitting in later layers to spurious correlations [1]). While the analysis sheds light on differences between networks trained with and without adversarial noise as well as their learning dynamics the findings are confined to the L-infinity definition of robustness studied for supervised image classification using ResNet-based architectures (for the main claims in section 4 and 5).

[1] “Last Layer Re-Training is Sufficient for Robustness to Spurious Correlations” [https://arxiv.org/abs/2204.02937](https://arxiv.org/abs/2204.02937)

included above

Limitations:
Yes.

# Hybrid Peer Review

## Summary

This paper investigates how representations learned by adversarially robust neural networks differ from those learned by standard non-robust networks. Using Centered Kernel Alignment (CKA) as the primary representation similarity metric, the authors conduct an empirical analysis across CIFAR-10, ImageNette, and ImageWoof datasets. The key findings are: (1) robust networks exhibit higher cross-layer similarity and lack the characteristic 'block structure' observed in non-robust networks; (2) early layers remain largely unaffected by adversarial perturbations even in non-robust networks; (3) deeper layers exhibit overfitting during robust training while early layers converge quickly; and (4) representations learned under different threat models (JPEG, Gabor, Snow) exhibit varying degrees of similarity. The authors propose these observations as diagnostic insights to guide future improvements in robust network design and training.

## Strengths

The paper addresses an important and timely question about understanding robust learning through a novel empirical lens. A primary strength is that the use of representation similarity metrics enables fine-grained layer-wise comparisons that aggregate metrics like accuracy and loss cannot provide, offering interpretable insights into how adversarial training shapes learned representations. The choice of CKA is well-motivated and justified with computational efficiency comparisons, and the authors acknowledge its known limitations.

The experimental scope is commendable and comprehensive. The authors systematically vary multiple factors including network width and depth, adversarial budget (ε), threat models, and datasets, with extensive ablations provided in the appendices. The findings demonstrate consistency across datasets and architectures, suggesting the observations are reasonably robust. The paper is well-written with clear visualizations that effectively communicate complex high-dimensional comparisons, and the experimental setup is clearly described with attention to reproducibility.

The specific finding regarding overfitting in deeper layers during robust training is particularly valuable and aligns with existing work relating overfitting in later layers to spurious correlations. More broadly, the paper's systematic documentation of how representation properties differ between robust and non-robust networks provides a useful empirical foundation for understanding adversarial training dynamics.

## Major Weaknesses and Limitations

### Scope and Generalizability

The authors' claims require more careful scoping. While the paper claims to investigate "DNNs with different architectures" (line 12) and the "impact of choice of architecture" (line 37), the experiments are restricted to ResNet-based architectures. A meaningful comparison of "different architectures" would encompass transformer-based architectures, MLP-based models, and other modern architectures such as Vision Transformers—increasingly important given their adoption in the community. The paper provides only brief discussion of whether findings apply to non-convolutional architectures. The authors should explicitly couch claims within the confines of ResNet-based models in the introduction, abstract, title, and conclusions.

Similarly, all experiments are restricted to image classification on relatively small datasets (CIFAR-10, and ImageNette/ImageWoof subsets of ImageNet rather than full ImageNet, due to computational constraints). The generalizability to full ImageNet, other domains (NLP, audio, graphs), or other learning paradigms (semi-supervised, self-supervised, or unsupervised robust learning) remains unclear and should be explicitly discussed. The paper does not explore whether findings extend to robustness against other challenges, such as robustness to common corruptions (blur, noise, weather) or certified robustness methods, which represent fundamentally different robustness challenges.

### Terminology and Precision

The term "robust" is used imprecisely throughout the work. "Robustness" is a broad concept in image classification—it can refer to robustness with respect to rendering methods (ImageNet-Sketch), artifacts such as blurring (ImageNet-C), adversarial examples (ImageNet-A), natural transformations (pose), or other perturbations. The authors intermix "robustness to adversarial examples" and use "robust network" to describe networks trained with robustness to L∞-bounded noise specifically. The authors should clarify terminology to explicitly denote that this work studies robustness to adversarial examples under L∞ perturbations, and avoid using "robust" as shorthand without qualification. This precision is important for readers seeking to understand whether findings apply to other robustness notions.

### Causal Analysis and Mechanistic Understanding

A significant limitation is that the paper is primarily observational and correlational rather than causal. While the authors acknowledge this limitation, the lack of causal analysis substantially limits the actionability of the findings. For instance, the paper identifies that robust networks lack specialization (block structure) and that deeper layers overfit, but does not establish whether these phenomena directly cause the performance degradation or are merely symptoms of other underlying optimization dynamics. The hypothesis that reduced "relative capacity" explains why increasing ε reduces block structure is interesting but underdeveloped and lacks rigorous justification. Why should increased task complexity necessarily manifest as higher cross-layer similarity rather than other representation changes?

The proposed improvements—layer freezing, layer-wise regularization to promote differentiation, and threat model selection based on representation similarity—are speculative and not empirically validated within the paper. This significantly reduces the practical impact. Can the authors provide evidence that artificially enforcing layer-wise differentiation during robust training would actually improve benign accuracy? Do early stopping strategies tailored to different layers (based on layer-wise convergence metrics) actually improve robustness-accuracy tradeoffs? Preliminary validation of even one proposed direction would strengthen the contribution.

### Metric Validation and Robustness

While CKA is computationally efficient and well-motivated, the main analysis relies heavily on a single similarity metric. Although Appendix B.1 mentions that SVCCA and Procrustes yield "similar high-level insights," this comparison lacks quantitative detail and rigor. The paper would be substantially strengthened by validating key findings across multiple representation similarity metrics (SVCCA, Procrustes, PWCCA) as primary metrics rather than relegating such comparisons to brief appendix mentions. Additionally, the paper does not address CKA's known limitations, such as insensitivity to principal component deletions, or validate that the online CKA approximation (with batch-size 1024 and 3 passes) does not introduce bias in similarity estimates. Sensitivity analysis on batch-size and kernel hyperparameters would strengthen confidence in the robustness of observations.

The paper reports CKA values without confidence intervals or statistical tests. Given that values are averaged over 3 runs, uncertainty estimates would strengthen claims about differences between robust and non-robust networks and help establish statistical significance of observed patterns.

### Limited Mechanistic Insight into Key Findings

Several interesting observations lack sufficient mechanistic explanation. For example, Section 6 finds that visually dissimilar threat models (Snow and Gabor) produce surprisingly similar robust representations, but provides no hypothesis grounded in signal properties or frequency analysis. Why would these attacks, which are visually distinct, lead to similar representations? The paper offers no explanation. Similarly, the transferability analysis (Section 4.2) observes poor transferability from robust to non-robust networks but does not rigorously establish whether this is due to representation differences or other factors such as decision boundary geometry.

## Minor Weaknesses

The comparison of representations under different threat models (Section 6) appears somewhat removed from the primary findings and is not particularly informative. While the observation about Snow and Gabor similarity is intriguing, the section lacks depth of analysis and mechanistic understanding, making it feel tangential to the main narrative.

The paper does not adequately discuss whether the observed representation properties are fundamental to the robust training objective or could be mitigated with different training procedures already in the literature. Related work on loss landscape geometry and implicit regularization in adversarial training could provide relevant context.

The paper would benefit from discussion of potential negative societal impacts. While adversarial robustness is generally beneficial, the insights could potentially inform adversary strategies, and the paper should acknowledge these tensions and any safeguards.

## Questions for Authors

1. Can you provide causal evidence that lack of specialization directly causes reduced benign accuracy? Have you attempted interventions (e.g., regularization promoting layer differentiation) to test this hypothesis experimentally?

2. How do your findings generalize to modern architectures beyond ResNets? Vision Transformers have fundamentally different inductive biases—do they exhibit block structure in non-robust training, and does it disappear under adversarial training?

3. Regarding the overfitting observation in deeper layers: How does this compare quantitatively to overfitting in non-robust networks? Is the difference statistically significant?

4. Can you validate key findings with alternative representation similarity metrics (SVCCA, Procrustes) as primary metrics, not just appendix comparisons? This would strengthen confidence that conclusions are robust to metric choice.

5. For the proposed layer freezing strategy: Have you tested whether freezing early layers during robust training actually improves training efficiency or final robustness?

6. Can you provide mechanistic insight into why visually dissimilar threat models (Snow and Gabor) produce similar robust representations? Does this insight extend to other visually diss

reviews.text:
# Hybrid Peer Review

## Summary

This paper investigates the properties of adversarially robust neural networks through the lens of representation similarity, using Centered Kernel Alignment (CKA) as the primary metric for analysis. The authors conduct a comprehensive empirical study across CIFAR-10, ImageNette, and ImageWoof datasets, examining how representations differ between robust and non-robust networks across multiple architectures, adversarial budgets, and threat models. Key findings include: (1) robust networks exhibit significantly higher cross-layer similarity and lack the characteristic 'block structure' observed in non-robust networks; (2) early layers remain largely unaffected by adversarial perturbations even in non-robust networks; (3) deeper layers exhibit overfitting during robust training while early layers converge quickly; and (4) visually dissimilar threat models can produce similar robust representations. The authors propose these observations as diagnostic insights to guide future improvements in robust network design and training.

## Strengths

1. **Novel and systematic perspective**: The paper addresses an important problem by analyzing robustness through representation similarity—a perspective that is intuitive yet provides interpretable layer-wise insights that aggregate metrics like accuracy cannot capture. The authors conduct a very systematic study with extensive experiments across multiple dimensions (architectures, adversarial budgets, threat models, datasets), yielding numerous interesting discoveries about what the authors term "salient pitfalls" in robust networks.

2. **Well-justified methodology**: The choice of CKA as the primary similarity metric is well-motivated with clear justification for computational efficiency and theoretical properties compared to alternatives. The paper demonstrates comprehensive experimental coverage with systematic ablations over network width, depth, and other architectural factors.

3. **Clear presentation and reproducibility**: The paper is well-written with logical organization and effective visualizations that communicate complex high-dimensional comparisons clearly. The authors support reproducibility by promising open-sourced code and providing detailed experimental setup including datasets, architectures, attack parameters, and GPU specifications.

4. **Consistent findings across conditions**: The observations appear robust across different datasets and architectures, suggesting generalizability within the studied domain. The extensive experimental scope, including detailed appendices with ablations, demonstrates thorough empirical validation of the main claims.

## Major Weaknesses

1. **Limited causal analysis and mechanistic understanding**: While the paper identifies interesting correlational patterns—such as the relationship between lack of specialization and benign accuracy loss, or the observation that deeper layers overfit during robust training—it does not establish causal relationships or provide mechanistic explanations for why these phenomena occur. The authors acknowledge that their findings reveal correlations rather than causal links, which significantly limits the actionability of the work. For instance, is the lack of specialization in robust networks a root cause of poor benign accuracy, or merely a symptom of other optimization dynamics? Why should increased task complexity from adversarial perturbations necessarily manifest as higher cross-layer similarity rather than other representation changes? The hypothesis about "relative capacity reduction" is interesting but underdeveloped and lacks rigorous justification.

2. **Single similarity metric with limited validation**: Although CKA is computationally efficient and theoretically motivated, the main analysis relies heavily on this one metric. While the authors mention that CKA yields "similar high-level insights" to SVCCA and Procrustes in Appendix B.1, this validation is limited in detail. The paper would be significantly strengthened by more thorough quantitative comparison showing that conclusions hold across multiple complementary metrics (SVCCA, Procrustes, PWCCA). Additionally, the paper acknowledges CKA's known limitations (e.g., insensitivity to principal component deletions) but does not adequately address whether these limitations affect the validity of the conclusions.

3. **Speculative proposed improvements without empirical validation**: The paper proposes several concrete directions for improvement (layer freezing, layer-wise regularization, threat model selection based on representation similarity) but does not experimentally validate any of these suggestions. The connection between observed representation properties and actionable improvements remains tenuous. For example, while the authors identify that deeper layers overfit during robust training, they do not test whether layer-wise early stopping strategies or layer-specific regularization actually improves robustness-accuracy tradeoffs. Similarly, the layer freezing experiments mentioned in Appendix D.5 are limited, and it remains unclear whether freezing early layers during adversarial training would improve both training efficiency and final performance.

4. **Limited scope and generalization concerns**: The experiments are restricted to image classification on relatively small datasets (CIFAR-10, ImageNette/Woof subsets of ImageNet rather than full ImageNet due to computational costs). This raises questions about whether findings generalize to larger-scale settings or other domains entirely. The paper does not adequately discuss generalization to other domains (NLP, audio, graphs) where representation learning dynamics may differ fundamentally. Additionally, experiments use primarily ResNets and Wide-ResNets; modern architectures like Vision Transformers are not analyzed, despite their increasing importance in the community. The paper briefly mentions but does not explore whether transformers exhibit similar representation properties or whether the lack of block structure also emerges under adversarial training of vision transformers.

## Minor Weaknesses and Questions

1. **Insufficient analysis of threat model similarity**: The observation that visually dissimilar attacks (Snow and Gabor) produce similar robust representations is intriguing but lacks mechanistic insight. Why do these visually different perturbations lead to similar representations? Could this finding be validated across other visually dissimilar pairs? The paper does not clarify the methodology for aligning perturbation budgets across visually different attacks or whether perceptual metrics were used to equate visual similarity.

2. **Incomplete coverage of robust training methods**: The paper focuses primarily on standard adversarial training and TRADES but does not analyze newer methods such as certified defenses, semi-supervised robust training, or recent architectural innovations (e.g., MART, AWP). It remains unclear whether the observed representation properties are specific to adversarial training or occur across different robustness training paradigms.

3. **Quantitative relationships not established**: While the paper links representation properties to performance degradation, the quantitative relationships are not rigorously established. How much does lack of specialization contribute to benign accuracy drop? Could you formalize the relationship between block structure strength and benign accuracy across different epsilon values? Statistical significance testing is also absent—CKA values are reported without confidence intervals despite being averaged over only 3 runs, which would strengthen claims about differences between robust and non-robust networks.

4. **Limited exploration of other robustness notions**: The paper focuses exclusively on adversarial robustness but does not explore whether findings extend to robustness against common corruptions (blur, noise, weather effects) or certified robustness methods. These represent different robustness challenges and may exhibit different representation properties.

5. **Transferability analysis could be deeper**: The observation that adversarial examples transfer poorly from robust to non-robust networks due to representation differences is interesting, but the paper does not rigorously establish causality. Could other factors such as decision boundary geometry play a role? Furthermore, the paper does not adequately explore whether the representation similarity insights could suggest defensive strategies against transfer attacks.

6. **Metric sensitivity and validation**: How sensitive are the findings to CKA kernel choice and hyperparameters? The use of online CKA with batch-size 1024 and 3 passes for computational efficiency is mentioned, but the paper does not validate that this approximation does not introduce bias in similarity estimates. Sensitivity analysis on batch-size would strengthen claims.

## Questions for Authors

1. Can you provide causal evidence that the lack of specialization in robust networks directly causes reduced benign accuracy? For instance, could you design regularizers that explicitly promote layer-wise differentiation and test whether this improves robust accuracy or the benign accuracy-robustness tradeoff?

2. Can you validate key findings with alternative representation similarity metrics (SVCCA, Procrustes) on a substantial subset of experiments to confirm the robustness of your observations to metric choice?

3. The observation that deeper layers overfit during robust training is correlational. Can you experimentally validate that layer-wise regularization or layer-specific early stopping strategies actually improve robustness-accuracy tradeoffs?

4. How do your findings generalize to Vision Transformers and other modern architectures? Do transformers exhibit similar block structure in non-robust training, and does it disappear under adversarial training?

5. For the layer freezing suggestion: Have you tested whether freezing early layers during robust training actually improves training efficiency or final robustness?

6. Can you formalize the "relative capacity reduction" hypothesis? Does increasing adversarial budget ε by a factor k have the same effect on representation similarity as reducing network width by factor k?

7. When comparing different threat models in Section 6, how did you align perturbation budgets across visually different attacks? Did you use perceptual metrics (e.g., LPIPS) to equate visual similarity?

8. Have you explored whether the reduced specialization in robust networks is due to lower effective capacity or represents a fundamental property of the min-max optimization objective?

## Limitations

The authors appropriately acknowledge several

reviews.summary:
This paper presents a probing analysis on clean (non-robust) vs. adversarially trained robust models. The paper's novelty is questionable and the insights gained are also sort of obvious, e.g., that the representational differences between inputs increase as one goes deeper down the layers. While this is true (and has been observed by the authors in their experiments), the authors didn't make an attempt to analyze whether two inputs from the same class are do have similar representations, which is a good thing. How does this compare between robust and non-robust models?

The paper introduces a lot of defence mechanisms (mainly centred around the min-max idea). However, it only employs PGD asdversarial training for obtaining a "robust" model. What about other approaches that provide defence mechanisms against potential attacks, e.g. the references 23, 32, 43 etc. that the authors themselves cite?

3. Some analysis could/should have been at a more detailed level, like one would still want the differences between different classes to be high and similarties between identical classes to be high. What observations can we make regarding this expected behavior in a robust and a non-robust network?
