title: LeRaC: Learning Rate Curriculum

reviews.summary: This paper proposes to modify neural net training by applying a different learning rate to each layer: the deeper layers are assigned a lower learning rate that progressively grows to match the first layer learning rate.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
The paper is clear, the approach is simple and can be implemented easily. The experiments shows better generalization when adopting the proposed learning rate schedule. The paper could be improved on two main axes:
(i) presenting the approach as an optimization method seem more appropriate than the curriculum learning angle, (ii) more analysis and justifications of why the method works seem necessary.
Overall, I feel that the paper does not explain how/if the proposed differentiated learning rate schedule is different from the effective learning rates reached by different settings of adaptive optimizers (momentum, Adadelta, Adam, SM3...).
reviews.questions:
== Optimization vs curriculum learning ==

The proposed method uses a different learning rate per layer. This endeavor has been widely explored in the literature on optimization of neural nets while the name curriculum learning is usually employed for methods modifying the ordering of the examples during training. I agree that the name curriculum could make sense in your context but the paper needs to spend more time on previous work on optimization. This means expanding the related work section and the empirical analysis.

The Adadelta paper (Zeiler 2012) observes the benefit to use different learning rates for different layers and Adadelta results in "effective learning rates [...] are larger for the lower layers of the network and much
smaller for the top layer at the beginning of training." Similar observations can be made for other adaptive learning rate algorithms like Adagrad, Adam, Adafactor, Adamax and AdamW or even later algorithms like e.g. SM3 (Memory-Efficient Adaptive Optimization, NeurIPS'19, see Figure 1.).

To justify your approach it seems necessary to compare your method at least with Adam and report the effective average learning rate (e.g. see Zeiler 2012's definition) per layer for both methods and how their parameters control the differentiation of learning rates across layers during training. The fact that your method is used in conjunction with Adam (or variants) could mean that either (i) the same type of learning rate schedule could be attained by changing Adam's parameters or (ii) your method's benefit comes from schedules unreachable by Adam. It seems very important to explore both methods' parameters to distinguish these two cases.

== Benefit is faster training or better generalization? ==

Modifying the training algorithm requires reporting training curves with *both* validation and training loss over time. The paper does not report any training loss which left us wonder if the generalization benefit comes from better optimization (lower training and validation loss) versus a better generalization trajectory (reaching a lower validation loss at the same training loss). 

== Schedule Tuning ==

Could you explain how the exponential scheduler of Eq. 8 has been selected? Is it the only option that you tried? How is the training speed affected by the choice of the additional hyperparameters (c, \nu^{(0)}_n, k)? In particular, compared to a robust baseline like Adam, would most hyperparameter choices yield faster or slower training? I do not understand how c can be set to 10 without validation.

== Number of training steps ==

The experimental section should spend more time describing how the number of training steps was selected. In which cases the baselines would reach the same or better validation loss with more steps? E.g. Figure 1.d. seems to suggest that better validation loss with CBS can be reached with more training. This improvement does not seem to agree with the sentence "all models are trained with early stopping", does it?

== Justification of the noise argument ==

Adaptive methods (Adam etc) justify higher learning rates for first layers with a curvature argument: in laymans term, to get the same impact on the output the first layers' updates require a larger learning rates than the last ones. Adadelta (Zeiler 2012) also mentions the larger learning rate in light of the vanishing gradient effect (Bengio 2014). Your motivation (from [21]) is to reduce the effect of "noise due to untrained parameters". This argument is unclear to the reader and it would help if you could find a way to measure/characterize that "noise" and show how your method reduces its effect.
reviews.limitations: This work does not raises concerns on societal impact.
reviews.rating: 6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper proposes to modify neural net training by applying a different learning rate to each layer: the deeper layers are assigned a lower learning rate that progressively grows to match the first layer learning rate.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
The paper is clear, the approach is simple and can be implemented easily. The experiments shows better generalization when adopting the proposed learning rate schedule. The paper could be improved on two main axes:
(i) presenting the approach as an optimization method seem more appropriate than the curriculum learning angle, (ii) more analysis and justifications of why the method works seem necessary.
Overall, I feel that the paper does not explain how/if the proposed differentiated learning rate schedule is different from the effective learning rates reached by different settings of adaptive optimizers (momentum, Adadelta, Adam, SM3...).

Questions:
== Optimization vs curriculum learning ==

The proposed method uses a different learning rate per layer. This endeavor has been widely explored in the literature on optimization of neural nets while the name curriculum learning is usually employed for methods modifying the ordering of the examples during training. I agree that the name curriculum could make sense in your context but the paper needs to spend more time on previous work on optimization. This means expanding the related work section and the empirical analysis.

The Adadelta paper (Zeiler 2012) observes the benefit to use different learning rates for different layers and Adadelta results in "effective learning rates [...] are larger for the lower layers of the network and much
smaller for the top layer at the beginning of training." Similar observations can be made for other adaptive learning rate algorithms like Adagrad, Adam, Adafactor, Adamax and AdamW or even later algorithms like e.g. SM3 (Memory-Efficient Adaptive Optimization, NeurIPS'19, see Figure 1.).

To justify your approach it seems necessary to compare your method at least with Adam and report the effective average learning rate (e.g. see Zeiler 2012's definition) per layer for both methods and how their parameters control the differentiation of learning rates across layers during training. The fact that your method is used in conjunction with Adam (or variants) could mean that either (i) the same type of learning rate schedule could be attained by changing Adam's parameters or (ii) your method's benefit comes from schedules unreachable by Adam. It seems very important to explore both methods' parameters to distinguish these two cases.

== Benefit is faster training or better generalization? ==

Modifying the training algorithm requires reporting training curves with *both* validation and training loss over time. The paper does not report any training loss which left us wonder if the generalization benefit comes from better optimization (lower training and validation loss) versus a better generalization trajectory (reaching a lower validation loss at the same training loss). 

== Schedule Tuning ==

Could you explain how the exponential scheduler of Eq. 8 has been selected? Is it the only option that you tried? How is the training speed affected by the choice of the additional hyperparameters (c, \nu^{(0)}_n, k)? In particular, compared to a robust baseline like Adam, would most hyperparameter choices yield faster or slower training? I do not understand how c can be set to 10 without validation.

== Number of training steps ==

The experimental section should spend more time describing how the number of training steps was selected. In which cases the baselines would reach the same or better validation loss with more steps? E.g. Figure 1.d. seems to suggest that better validation loss with CBS can be reached with more training. This improvement does not seem to agree with the sentence "all models are trained with early stopping", does it?

== Justification of the noise argument ==

Adaptive methods (Adam etc) justify higher learning rates for first layers with a curvature argument: in laymans term, to get the same impact on the output the first layers' updates require a larger learning rates than the last ones. Adadelta (Zeiler 2012) also mentions the larger learning rate in light of the vanishing gradient effect (Bengio 2014). Your motivation (from [21]) is to reduce the effect of "noise due to untrained parameters". This argument is unclear to the reader and it would help if you could find a way to measure/characterize that "noise" and show how your method reduces its effect.

Limitations:
This work does not raises concerns on societal impact.

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
# Hybrid Peer Review

## Summary

This paper proposes LeRaC (Learning Rate Curriculum), a model-level curriculum learning approach that assigns different learning rates to each neural network layer during early training epochs. Specifically, layers closer to the input receive higher learning rates, which gradually increase until all layers converge to a uniform learning rate after k epochs (typically 2-7). The authors argue this creates a data-free curriculum that mitigates noise from random weight initialization. The method is evaluated on eight datasets across three domains (vision, NLP, audio) with diverse architectures (ResNets, DenseNet, BERT, transformers, LSTMs), demonstrating consistent improvements over both standard training and the state-of-the-art Curriculum by Smoothing (CBS) method, with no additional computational overhead.

## Strengths

The paper presents a clear, simple approach that is easy to implement and broadly applicable across different architectures and domains without requiring domain-specific modifications. The experimental evaluation is comprehensive, spanning three domains with multiple architectures and proper statistical reporting (5 runs with standard deviations). A notable strength is the consistency of improvements—unlike CBS, which shows mixed results, LeRaC improves performance across all tested configurations. The method is also computationally efficient, adding no overhead compared to standard training, which is a practical advantage over CBS. The presentation is generally clear and the mathematical formulation (Equations 5-8) is well-articulated.

## Major Weaknesses

### 1. Weak Theoretical Justification and Unclear Motivation

The paper's core claim that "noise level grows with each layer" (lines 175-176) is presented as conjecture rather than rigorously validated. The motivation drawn from reference [21] regarding "noise due to untrained parameters" is unclear and lacks empirical characterization. The paper would substantially benefit from measuring or characterizing this noise—for instance, through gradient norms, activation statistics, or other metrics—and demonstrating how the proposed method specifically reduces its effect. Without this, the connection between per-layer learning rate scheduling and noise mitigation remains intuitive but unsubstantiated.

More fundamentally, the paper does not adequately explain how/if the proposed differentiated learning rate schedule differs from effective learning rates achieved by adaptive optimizers. This is a critical omission. The Adadelta paper (Zeiler 2012) already observed that different learning rates for different layers provide benefits, resulting in "effective learning rates [that] are larger for the lower layers of the network and much smaller for the top layer at the beginning of training." Similar observations apply to Adagrad, Adam, Adafactor, Adamax, AdamW, and more recent methods like SM3. Since the paper uses LeRaC in conjunction with Adam or variants, it remains unclear whether: (i) the same type of learning rate schedule could be attained by changing Adam's parameters, or (ii) LeRaC's benefits come from schedules unreachable by Adam. This distinction is very important and requires careful empirical analysis comparing effective learning rates per layer for both methods.

### 2. Positioning and Framing Issues

The paper presents the approach as curriculum learning, but this framing is problematic. Curriculum learning typically refers to methods that modify the ordering or weighting of training examples from easy to hard. The proposed method, by contrast, modifies per-layer learning rates—an endeavor widely explored in optimization literature. The paper should be repositioned as an optimization method rather than curriculum learning. This requires substantially expanding the related work section to engage with the extensive literature on layer-wise learning rate adaptation, including methods like LARS and LAMB, which also adjust per-layer learning rates. The current related work section insufficiently addresses this prior work, making it difficult to assess the novelty of the contribution.

### 3. Incomplete Experimental Analysis

A critical gap is the absence of training loss curves. The paper reports only validation loss, making it impossible to determine whether generalization improvements stem from better optimization (lower training and validation loss) or from a better generalization trajectory (reaching lower validation loss at the same training loss). Reporting both training and validation loss is essential for understanding the method's mechanism.

Additionally, the experimental section inadequately describes how the number of training steps was selected. In particular, the paper claims "all models are trained with early stopping," yet Figure 1.d suggests that CBS could reach comparable or better validation loss with more training steps. This apparent contradiction needs clarification. A systematic analysis of whether baselines would reach equivalent performance with extended training is necessary to properly assess LeRaC's benefits.

### 4. Hyperparameter Selection and Sensitivity

The paper introduces multiple hyperparameters (k, η₁⁽⁰⁾, ηₙ⁽⁰⁾, c) that require tuning. Table 1 reveals substantial variation across architectures (k ranges from 2-7 epochs, learning rate ratios span multiple orders of magnitude), raising serious questions about the claimed simplicity and universality of the method. The paper provides only vague guidance ("5-6 orders of magnitude") for practitioners setting these values.

Critically, the exponential scheduler (Equation 8) appears to be selected without justification. The paper does not explain why exponential scheduling was chosen or whether it is the only option attempted. The comparison with linear scheduling (Table 5) is limited to a single architecture and shows only marginal differences, yet the exponential choice is presented without adequate ablation studies. How does the choice of the exponential base c=10 affect results? How sensitive is performance to k across different architectures? Without systematic sensitivity analysis, claims of robustness are undermined. Furthermore, it is unclear how c can be set to 10 without validation, contradicting the data-free motivation.

### 5. Modest and Potentially Insignificant Improvements

Many reported improvements are small, often in the range of 0.3-0.7%, with some within the reported standard deviation ranges. For example, on CIFAR-10 with ResNet-18, the improvement is 0.36% (89.20±0.43 → 89.56±0.16), where confidence intervals substantially overlap. The paper does not provide statistical significance testing (e.g., t-tests) to establish that these improvements are meaningful beyond noise. Given the small margins and reported standard deviations, it is unclear whether the gains represent genuine improvements or fall within experimental noise. This raises questions about practical significance: would practitioners observe consistent benefits across their own experimental runs?

### 6. Limited Baseline Comparisons

The paper compares primarily against CBS and standard training, but omits comparisons with other important baselines. Most critically, there is no comparison with adaptive learning rate methods (Adam, AdamW, etc.) that already achieve per-layer learning rate differentiation through their own mechanisms. How does LeRaC compare to layer-wise learning rate adaptation in modern optimizers like LARS or LAMB? Do these methods already achieve similar benefits? The paper should also compare against other recent model-level curriculum approaches beyond CBS, such as self-paced learning or teacher-student methods, to properly situate the contribution.

Additionally, some experimental results raise concerns. For instance, the CBS results for pre-trained CvT-13 on CIFAR-100 (85.85% vs. 94.15% for conventional training) are anomalous and suggest potential implementation or hyperparameter tuning issues that should be clarified.

## Minor Weaknesses and Questions

1. **Architectural insights**: Which layers contribute most to performance gains? Does the benefit concentrate in early, middle, or late layers? Layer-wise analysis would validate the noise propagation hypothesis.

2. **Interaction with modern techniques**: How does LeRaC interact with batch normalization, layer normalization, and dropout, which may already mitigate initialization noise? Ablation studies exploring these interactions would strengthen the paper.

3. **Scalability**: Have you tested on very deep networks (ResNet-152, ViT-Large) or modern large language models? Does the method scale effectively?

4. **Failure modes**: The paper acknowledges no significant limitations (Checklist 1b), which is implausible. Under what conditions does LeRaC fail or underperform? When should practitioners avoid this method?

5. **Connection to curriculum learning**: How exactly does assigning lower learning rates to deeper layers create a curriculum in the traditional sense (easy-to-hard progression)? The conceptual link feels loose compared to data-level curriculum learning.

## Limitations

The paper's limitations section is inadequate. The authors claim to have identified no significant limitations (Checklist 1b), which is concerning for a paper with the issues identified above. Key unaddressed limitations include:

- The theoretical gap between the motivation (noise mitigation) and the proposed solution (per-layer learning rate scheduling) remains unvalidated.
- The method's success appears dependent on careful hyperparameter tuning, but practitioners receive limited principled guidance for setting these values without validation data.
- The framing as curriculum learning is questionable; the method may be better understood as a regularization or optimization technique.
- Improvements are often marginal and lack statistical significance testing despite overlapping confidence intervals.
- The paper does not discuss

This means expanding the related work section and the empirical analysis.

The Adadelta paper (Zeiler 2012) observes the benefit to use different learning rates for different layers and Adadelta results in "effective learning rates [...] are larger for the lower layers of the network and much
smaller for the top layer at the beginning of training." Similar observations can be made for other adaptive learning rate algorithms like Adagrad, Adam, Adafactor, Adamax and AdamW or even later algorithms like e.g. SM3 (Memory-Efficient Adaptive Optimization, NeurIPS'19, see Figure 1.).

To justify your approach it seems necessary to compare your method at least with Adam and report the effective average learning rate (e.g. see Zeiler 2012's definition) per layer for both methods and how their parameters control the differentiation of learning rates across layers during training. The fact that your method is used in conjunction with Adam (or variants) could mean that either (i) the same type of learning rate schedule could be attained by changing Adam's parameters or (ii) your method's benefit comes from schedules unreachable by Adam. It seems very important to explore both methods' parameters to distinguish these two cases.

== Benefit is faster training or better generalization? ==

Modifying the training algorithm requires reporting training curves with *both* validation and training loss over time. The paper does not report any training loss which left us wonder if the generalization benefit comes from better optimization (lower training and validation loss) versus a better generalization trajectory (reaching a lower validation loss at the same training loss). 

== Schedule Tuning ==

Could you explain how the exponential scheduler of Eq. 8 has been selected? Is it the only option that you tried? How is the training speed affected by the choice of the additional hyperparameters (c, \nu^{(0)}_n, k)? In particular, compared to a robust baseline like Adam, would most hyperparameter choices yield faster or slower training? I do not understand how c can be set to 10 without validation.

== Number of training steps ==

The experimental section should spend more time describing how the number of training steps was selected. In which cases the baselines would reach the same or better validation loss with more steps? E.g. Figure 1.d. seems to suggest that better validation loss with CBS can be reached with more training. This improvement does not seem to agree with the sentence "all models are trained with early stopping", does it?

== Justification of the noise argument ==

Adaptive methods (Adam etc) justify higher learning rates for first layers with a curvature argument: in laymans term, to get the same impact on the output the first layers' updates require a larger learning rates than the last ones. Adadelta (Zeiler 2012) also mentions the larger learning rate in light of the vanishing gradient effect (Bengio 2014). Your motivation (from [21]) is to reduce the effect of "noise due to untrained parameters". This argument is unclear to the reader and it would help if you could find a way to measure/characterize that "noise" and show how your method reduces its effect.

Limitations:
This work does not raises concerns on societal impact.

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

Summary:
This paper proposes setting different learning rates for each layer of a deep neural network such that the first layers (closer to the input) have a much higher learning rate than the last layers. Learning rates are all gradually increased until they reach the same value $\eta_0$ (the optimal learning rate of no-curriculum models).

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strengths:
- Simple, novel, and effective method. 
- Good review of related work and reasoning for using model-level curriculum learning.
- Consistent performance improvements across eight audio, image, and text datasets and seven convolutional, recurrent, and transformer architectures. 


Weaknesses:
- Insufficient analysis on why the method works. 
- The motivation for setting different learning rates per layer is not sound. The authors state that random initialization of parameters causes the propagation of noise during the forward pass, and that assigning higher learning rate to first layers and lower learning rates to last layers prevents this propagation of noise. But there is no proof for this argument.

Questions:
- What is the method for optimizing $\eta_j$, j = {1 ... n}?
- How sensitive is the model to different settings of $\eta_j$?
- How do the training dynamics compare to standard training where all layers are trained at the same rate? Would the earlier layers generate qualitatively different features maps than under-trained later layers?

Limitations:
- The interaction of the model with adaptive optimizers that scale the learning rate of each parameter is not well justified. 
- Learning rate schedulers may disturb the proposed model. If the base learning rate is reduced before the learning rate of the last layer $\eta_n$ is sufficiently increased, the last layer may never be trained enough, training may take much longer or may become unstable.

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
# Hybrid Peer Review: LeRaC - Learning Rate Curriculum

## Summary

This paper proposes LeRaC (Learning Rate Curriculum), a model-level curriculum learning approach that assigns different learning rates to each neural network layer during early training epochs. The method assigns higher learning rates to layers closer to the input and lower learning rates to deeper layers, with all rates gradually increasing until they converge to a common value over k epochs (typically 2-7). The authors argue this creates a data-free curriculum that mitigates noise from random weight initialization. LeRaC is evaluated comprehensively across eight datasets spanning vision (CIFAR-10/100, Tiny ImageNet), NLP (BoolQ, QNLI, RTE), and audio (ESC-50, CREMA-D) domains, using diverse architectures including ResNets, DenseNet, BERT, transformers, and LSTMs. Results demonstrate consistent improvements over standard training and generally superior performance compared to Curriculum by Smoothing (CBS), with no additional computational overhead.

## Strengths

The paper presents a **simple and elegant approach** that is easy to implement and broadly applicable across diverse architectures and domains without requiring domain-specific modifications or architectural changes. This generality is a significant practical advantage over methods like CBS, which rely on domain-specific smoothing operations.

**Comprehensive experimental evaluation** across three distinct domains with multiple architectures demonstrates genuine breadth. The paper includes proper statistical reporting with five runs and standard deviations, and the evaluation protocol appears sound. The method's **computational efficiency** is noteworthy—unlike CBS, LeRaC adds no training time overhead while achieving consistent improvements.

A key strength is **consistency**: LeRaC improves performance on all 12 image classification tasks and all 6 text classification tasks, whereas CBS shows inconsistent results (failing on 7/12 vision tasks in some configurations). This reliability is valuable for practitioners. The paper also includes a **clear presentation** of methodology with proper mathematical formulation and well-motivated reasoning for applying curriculum learning at the model level rather than the data level.

## Major Weaknesses

### Insufficient Theoretical Justification

The paper's core motivation rests on the claim that "noise level grows with each layer" (lines 175-176), but this remains a **conjecture without rigorous support**. While the intuition is reasonable—that random initialization propagates noise through the forward pass and that assigning higher learning rates to early layers prevents this propagation—the paper provides no formal analysis or empirical evidence to validate this hypothesis. Critically, the authors offer no proof that lower learning rates for deeper layers specifically address initialization noise better than alternative approaches.

The connection between this noise propagation argument and why per-layer learning rate scheduling constitutes curriculum learning is also tenuous. The paper does not rigorously explain how assigning lower learning rates to deeper layers creates a curriculum in the traditional sense of an easy-to-hard progression. This appears more like a form of adaptive regularization than classical curriculum learning, and the distinction deserves clarification.

### Marginal and Statistically Questionable Improvements

While improvements are consistent, many are modest and raise concerns about practical significance. For example, on CIFAR-10 with ResNet-18, the improvement is only 0.36% (89.20±0.43 vs. 89.56±0.16). Notably, the standard deviations overlap substantially in many cases, suggesting improvements may fall within noise margins. **The paper reports no statistical significance testing** despite these small margins, which is a critical omission. Without significance tests, it is difficult to assess whether observed improvements represent genuine gains or statistical artifacts.

### Hyperparameter Sensitivity and Tuning Burden

Despite claims of simplicity and universality, the method introduces multiple hyperparameters requiring careful tuning: k (curriculum duration), η₁⁽⁰⁾ and ηₙ⁽⁰⁾ (initial learning rates), and c (exponential base). Table 1 reveals **substantial per-architecture variation**: k ranges from 2-7 epochs, learning rate ratios span 5-6 orders of magnitude, and different optimizers and batch sizes are used across configurations. This variation directly contradicts the "data-free" and "universal" framing.

The paper provides only vague guidance for practitioners ("5-6 orders of magnitude difference"), with no principled method for setting these hyperparameters without extensive validation. This hyperparameter sensitivity significantly undermines the claimed simplicity and raises questions about how practitioners should apply LeRaC to new architectures or datasets without substantial tuning effort.

### Incomplete Analysis and Limited Novelty

The paper lacks sufficient **mechanistic understanding** of why the method works. No visualizations of loss curves, feature maps, or gradient flow analysis are provided to support the noise mitigation hypothesis. The comparison of linear versus exponential scheduling (Table 5) is limited to a single architecture and shows only marginal differences, yet the universal adoption of exponential scheduling with c=10 appears arbitrary and unjustified. Why is the exponential base universally optimal? How sensitive are results to this choice?

Additionally, **using per-layer learning rates is not entirely novel**—layer-wise learning rate adaptation appears in prior work and modern optimizers. The paper's contribution is primarily reframing this technique as curriculum learning, which feels incremental. The paper also lacks comparison with other per-layer adaptive methods such as LARS, LAMB, or layer-wise learning rate scheduling in existing optimizers, making it difficult to assess the novelty and advantage of LeRaC specifically.

### Insufficient Ablation Studies

The paper provides limited ablation studies beyond the linear vs. exponential comparison. Critical questions remain unanswered: How sensitive is performance to different initial learning rate ratios? Why are 5-6 orders of magnitude optimal? How does performance vary with network depth, and does the benefit diminish for very deep networks? Which layers contribute most to performance gains? These analyses would either validate the noise propagation hypothesis or reveal that the method's success stems from other factors.

## Minor Weaknesses and Concerns

**Incomplete baseline comparisons**: Only CBS is compared among model-level curriculum methods. Other recent curriculum learning approaches (self-paced learning, teacher-student methods, other model-level strategies) should be included. The paper also does not discuss when and why LeRaC might fail—CBS sometimes outperforms LeRaC (e.g., ResNet-18 on CIFAR-100: 72.64% vs. 72.90%), but there is minimal discussion of failure modes or architectural properties that determine success.

**Interaction with modern training techniques**: The paper does not adequately address how LeRaC interacts with batch normalization, layer normalization, or adaptive optimizers (Adam, AdamW). These techniques may already mitigate initialization noise, potentially reducing LeRaC's benefit. Additionally, the interaction with learning rate schedulers is concerning: if the base learning rate is reduced before the learning rate of the last layer is sufficiently increased, the last layer may be undertrained, potentially causing instability or extended training times.

**Anomalous results**: The pre-trained CvT-13 with CBS achieves 85.85% on CIFAR-100 versus 94.15% for conventional training, suggesting potential implementation issues or hyperparameter tuning problems that warrant clarification.

**Limited scope of experiments**: All experiments use relatively small models and datasets. Scalability to very large models (>1B parameters) or modern large language models remains unclear. Whether the noise hypothesis holds at different scales is unknown.

**Acknowledged limitations**: The authors state they found no significant limitations (checklist item 1b), which is implausible. Every method has limitations, and the paper should discuss when practitioners should and should not apply LeRaC, potential negative impacts from increased hyperparameter tuning complexity, and failure scenarios.

## Questions for Authors

1. Can you provide **empirical evidence** (e.g., gradient norms, activation statistics, noise measurements at different depths) that noise grows monotonically through layers during early training? How does this compare across different initialization schemes (He, Xavier, etc.)?

2. How were the **specific hyperparameter ranges** (5-6 orders of magnitude difference, c=10, k∈[2,7]) determined? Are these universally optimal or dataset/architecture-dependent? Sensitivity analysis would strengthen claims of universality.

3. Have you performed **statistical significance testing** on reported improvements? Many gains fall within or near standard deviation ranges.

4. Why is the **exponential scheduler** (Eq. 8) optimal compared to alternatives? Table 5 shows marginal differences with linear scheduling—is the added complexity justified?

5. How does LeRaC compare to **other per-layer adaptive methods** (LARS, LAMB, layer-wise learning rate adaptation)? Why were these not included as baselines?

6. Can you provide **principled guidelines** for selecting k and learning rate ranges for new architectures/datasets without extensive hyperparameter search?

7. How does LeRaC **interact with normalization techniques** (batch normalization, layer normalization) that may already address initialization noise?

Summary:
This paper proposes setting different learning rates for each layer of a deep neural network such that the first layers (closer to the input) have a much higher learning rate than the last layers. Learning rates are all gradually increased until they reach the same value $\eta_0$ (the optimal learning rate of no-curriculum models).

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strengths:
- Simple, novel, and effective method. 
- Good review of related work and reasoning for using model-level curriculum learning.
- Consistent performance improvements across eight audio, image, and text datasets and seven convolutional, recurrent, and transformer architectures. 


Weaknesses:
- Insufficient analysis on why the method works. 
- The motivation for setting different learning rates per layer is not sound. The authors state that random initialization of parameters causes the propagation of noise during the forward pass, and that assigning higher learning rate to first layers and lower learning rates to last layers prevents this propagation of noise. But there is no proof for this argument.

Questions:
- What is the method for optimizing $\eta_j$, j = {1 ... n}?
- How sensitive is the model to different settings of $\eta_j$?
- How do the training dynamics compare to standard training where all layers are trained at the same rate? Would the earlier layers generate qualitatively different features maps than under-trained later layers?

Limitations:
- The interaction of the model with adaptive optimizers that scale the learning rate of each parameter is not well justified. 
- Learning rate schedulers may disturb the proposed model. If the base learning rate is reduced before the learning rate of the last layer $\eta_n$ is sufficiently increased, the last layer may never be trained enough, training may take much longer or may become unstable.

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

reviews.summary: The paper proposes a kind of model-level curriculum learning strategy, which assigns higher initial learning rates to shallow layers than deep ones and continues increasing all learning rates until they reach the same value during the training process. It is a model- and task-agnostic approach. To verify its effectiveness, the authors apply it to multiple domains and different neural networks.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths:

1.The proposed strategy concerning layer-wise learning rate is important.

2.The empirical experiments are sufficient and comprehensive to prove the generality and effectiveness of the strategy.

3.This paper is well written and easy to read.

Weaknesses:

1.The relation between this work and curriculum learning is vague.  Curriculum learning is a training strategy that learns from easy to hard, or more generally, in a certain kind of meaningful order. For example, CBS, which is mentioned in the paper, anneals the standard deviation of Gaussian kernels to pass more high-frequency information. However, the relation between this work and CL is not so clear.

2.The discussed related work is not sufficient. Since this work focuses on the layer-wise learning rate, it should be discussed more clearly about how this work is different from the works about layer-wise(or adaptive) learning rate[1][2][5], which is a common and widely adopted strategy. Additionally, training with increasing learning rate in the early stages is a kind of warmup[3][4], the relations between this work and warmup strategies are required to discussed.  The vague position of this work makes its contribution vague.

3.This paper lacks theoretical analysis and does not explain why increasing the learning rates until reaching the same value is reasonable. It is not convincing only with the empirical results. The designed mechanism is neither intuitive nor well theoretically supported, making its justification questionable. The only way for justification is through experiments, which may be tricky, because the proposed method needs more hyper-parameters than that of the conventional methods.

[1] Singh, B., De, S., Zhang, Y., Goldstein, T., & Taylor, G. (2015, December). Layer-specific adaptive learning rates for deep networks. In 2015 IEEE 14th International Conference on Machine Learning and Applications (ICMLA) (pp. 364-368). IEEE.

[2] Ginsburg, B., Gitman, I., & You, Y. (2018). Large batch training of convolutional networks with layer-wise adaptive rate scaling.

[3] Gotmare, A., Keskar, N. S., Xiong, C., & Socher, R. (2018). A closer look at deep learning heuristics: Learning rate restarts, warmup and distillation. 

[4] You, Y., Gitman, I., & Ginsburg, B. (2017). Large batch training of convolutional networks. 

[5] Adam:A Method for Stochastic Optimization. ICLR 2015
reviews.questions:
1. There are many hyperparameters of this strategy, so I’d like to know how to choose proper initial learning rates for all layers and how does the sensitivity of hyperparameters affect the experimental results?
2. For pre-trained models, this paper states that “The same issue can occur if the weights are pre-trained on a distinct task, where the misalignment of the weights with a new task is likely higher for the high-level feature layers. ” However, it is generally believed that the learning rates of the layers close to the outputs are relatively high and the learning rates of the layers close to the inputs are low or even equals to zero in the process of fine-tuning, which seems to be the opposite of the strategy proposed in this paper. How to explain the difference between them?
3. It may be easier to understand Eq. (8), if the authors can plot a figure for μ(j).
4. Whether assigning higher learning rate to the deeper layers can also obtain better performance through hyper-parameter tuning ? The learning landscape of the models is not clear.
reviews.limitations: The authors do not discuss the limitation. However, there is a lack of both an intuitive explanation or theoretical analysis for the proposed method, making it hard to be a convincing work. A large line of related works is missed, making the contributions vague.
reviews.rating: 5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The paper proposes a kind of model-level curriculum learning strategy, which assigns higher initial learning rates to shallow layers than deep ones and continues increasing all learning rates until they reach the same value during the training process. It is a model- and task-agnostic approach. To verify its effectiveness, the authors apply it to multiple domains and different neural networks.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strengths:

1.The proposed strategy concerning layer-wise learning rate is important.

2.The empirical experiments are sufficient and comprehensive to prove the generality and effectiveness of the strategy.

3.This paper is well written and easy to read.

Weaknesses:

1.The relation between this work and curriculum learning is vague.  Curriculum learning is a training strategy that learns from easy to hard, or more generally, in a certain kind of meaningful order. For example, CBS, which is mentioned in the paper, anneals the standard deviation of Gaussian kernels to pass more high-frequency information. However, the relation between this work and CL is not so clear.

2.The discussed related work is not sufficient. Since this work focuses on the layer-wise learning rate, it should be discussed more clearly about how this work is different from the works about layer-wise(or adaptive) learning rate[1][2][5], which is a common and widely adopted strategy. Additionally, training with increasing learning rate in the early stages is a kind of warmup[3][4], the relations between this work and warmup strategies are required to discussed.  The vague position of this work makes its contribution vague.

3.This paper lacks theoretical analysis and does not explain why increasing the learning rates until reaching the same value is reasonable. It is not convincing only with the empirical results. The designed mechanism is neither intuitive nor well theoretically supported, making its justification questionable. The only way for justification is through experiments, which may be tricky, because the proposed method needs more hyper-parameters than that of the conventional methods.

[1] Singh, B., De, S., Zhang, Y., Goldstein, T., & Taylor, G. (2015, December). Layer-specific adaptive learning rates for deep networks. In 2015 IEEE 14th International Conference on Machine Learning and Applications (ICMLA) (pp. 364-368). IEEE.

[2] Ginsburg, B., Gitman, I., & You, Y. (2018). Large batch training of convolutional networks with layer-wise adaptive rate scaling.

[3] Gotmare, A., Keskar, N. S., Xiong, C., & Socher, R. (2018). A closer look at deep learning heuristics: Learning rate restarts, warmup and distillation. 

[4] You, Y., Gitman, I., & Ginsburg, B. (2017). Large batch training of convolutional networks. 

[5] Adam:A Method for Stochastic Optimization. ICLR 2015

Questions:
1. There are many hyperparameters of this strategy, so I’d like to know how to choose proper initial learning rates for all layers and how does the sensitivity of hyperparameters affect the experimental results?
2. For pre-trained models, this paper states that “The same issue can occur if the weights are pre-trained on a distinct task, where the misalignment of the weights with a new task is likely higher for the high-level feature layers. ” However, it is generally believed that the learning rates of the layers close to the outputs are relatively high and the learning rates of the layers close to the inputs are low or even equals to zero in the process of fine-tuning, which seems to be the opposite of the strategy proposed in this paper. How to explain the difference between them?
3. It may be easier to understand Eq. (8), if the authors can plot a figure for μ(j).
4. Whether assigning higher learning rate to the deeper layers can also obtain better performance through hyper-parameter tuning ? The learning landscape of the models is not clear.

Limitations:
The authors do not discuss the limitation. However, there is a lack of both an intuitive explanation or theoretical analysis for the proposed method, making it hard to be a convincing work. A large line of related works is missed, making the contributions vague.

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.text:
# Hybrid Peer Review: Learning Rate Curriculum (LeRaC)

## Summary

This paper proposes LeRaC (Learning Rate Curriculum), a model-level curriculum learning strategy that assigns different learning rates to neural network layers during early training epochs. Specifically, layers closer to the input receive higher initial learning rates than deeper layers, with all learning rates gradually increasing until they converge to a uniform value over k epochs (typically 2-7). The authors argue this creates a data-free curriculum that mitigates noise from random weight initialization. The method is evaluated comprehensively across eight datasets spanning three domains (vision, NLP, audio) and multiple architectures (ResNets, DenseNet, BERT, CvT, LSTM, transformers), compared against standard training and Curriculum by Smoothing (CBS). Results demonstrate consistent improvements with no additional computational overhead.

## Strengths

1. **Simplicity and Generality**: The approach is elegantly straightforward—modifying per-layer learning rates requires minimal implementation effort and is architecture-agnostic and task-agnostic. Unlike CBS, which requires domain-specific smoothing operations, LeRaC applies uniformly across CNNs, RNNs, and Transformers without architectural modifications.

2. **Comprehensive and Consistent Experimental Evaluation**: The paper includes extensive experiments across three distinct domains with diverse architectures and proper statistical reporting (5 runs with standard deviations). Critically, LeRaC shows consistent improvements across all 12 image classification tasks and all 6 text classification tasks, whereas CBS exhibits inconsistent results with failures on multiple configurations. This consistency is a valuable practical property.

3. **Computational Efficiency**: The method adds no training time overhead compared to standard training, unlike CBS which requires additional smoothing operations. This is an important practical advantage for widespread adoption.

4. **Clear Presentation**: The paper is well-written with clear motivation, mathematical formulation (Equations 5-8), and methodology that is easy to follow.

## Major Weaknesses

1. **Weak Theoretical Justification and Unclear Conceptual Foundation**: The paper lacks rigorous theoretical analysis explaining why the proposed learning rate schedule is reasonable. The core claim that "noise grows monotonically through layers" (lines 171-172) is stated as conjecture without formal validation or empirical evidence. The authors provide intuition about noise propagation from random initialization but do not explain *why* lower learning rates for deeper layers specifically address this problem better than alternatives. Critically, the connection between this work and curriculum learning is vague. Traditional curriculum learning involves learning from easy to hard or in meaningful order (e.g., CBS anneals Gaussian kernel standard deviations to control frequency information). However, the relationship between assigning lower learning rates to deeper layers and the concept of curriculum learning is not clearly established. This appears to be more of an adaptive regularization technique or layer-wise learning rate scheduling rather than curriculum learning in the classical sense. The paper would benefit from either: (a) providing formal theoretical analysis of the noise mitigation mechanism, (b) offering empirical evidence (e.g., gradient norm analysis, activation statistics) that noise indeed accumulates monotonically through layers, or (c) reconceptualizing the framing to move away from curriculum learning terminology.

2. **Insufficient Positioning Relative to Existing Work**: The paper's contribution is vague because it inadequately discusses how this work differs from established layer-wise and adaptive learning rate strategies. The related work section fails to adequately address:
   - **Layer-wise learning rate adaptation**: Prior work (Singh et al. 2015, Ginsburg et al. 2018) has explored layer-specific adaptive learning rates. How is LeRaC fundamentally different from these approaches?
   - **Warmup strategies**: The paper mentions that "training with increasing learning rate in the early stages" relates to warmup (lines 3-4), but does not clearly delineate the relationship. Modern training commonly uses learning rate warmup (Gotmare et al. 2018, You et al. 2017), which also gradually increases learning rates early in training. What distinguishes LeRaC from these established warmup strategies?
   - **Adaptive optimizers**: How does LeRaC interact with modern optimizers like Adam (Kingma & Ba 2015) that already perform per-parameter learning rate adaptation? The paper does not compare against layer-wise learning rate scheduling methods (e.g., LARS, LAMB) that also adjust per-layer learning rates.

   This insufficient positioning makes the novelty and contribution of the work unclear.

3. **Modest Empirical Improvements with Insufficient Statistical Validation**: While consistent, many improvements are small (often <1%), with several falling within or near the standard deviation ranges of baseline results. For example, on CIFAR-10 with ResNet-18, the improvement is 0.36% (89.20±0.43 baseline vs. 89.56±0.16 LeRaC)—the standard deviations overlap substantially. The paper reports results across multiple runs but does not conduct statistical significance testing (e.g., t-tests). Given the marginal gains and reported variance, it is unclear whether these improvements are statistically significant or merely within noise margins. This is a critical gap, as small improvements without statistical validation undermine the practical value of the method.

4. **Substantial Hyperparameter Tuning Burden Undermines Claims of Universality**: Despite claims of simplicity and universality, the method introduces multiple hyperparameters requiring careful tuning:
   - **k** (curriculum duration): ranges from 2-7 epochs across architectures (Table 1)
   - **η₁⁽⁰⁾ and ηₙ⁽⁰⁾** (initial learning rates): span 5-6 orders of magnitude with no principled guidance for selection
   - **c** (exponential base): fixed at 10 universally, but sensitivity to this choice is not analyzed
   - Additional dependencies on optimizer, batch size, and learning rate ranges vary substantially across architectures

   Table 1 reveals extensive per-architecture tuning, contradicting the paper's framing of LeRaC as parameter-free or universal. The paper provides only vague guidance ("5-6 orders of magnitude difference") rather than a principled method to set hyperparameters without validation data. This raises the question: How should practitioners select these hyperparameters for new architectures or datasets? The "data-free" claim becomes questionable if practitioners must conduct hyperparameter search on their target datasets.

5. **Incomplete Ablation Studies and Mechanistic Understanding**: While Table 5 compares linear versus exponential updates, deeper investigation is lacking:
   - Why is exponential scheduling (Equation 8) optimal? The comparison shows marginal differences between exponential and linear updates, questioning whether the added complexity of exponential scheduling is justified.
   - How sensitive is performance to the exponential base c=10? No sensitivity analysis is provided.
   - How were the 5-6 orders of magnitude difference between η₁ and ηₙ determined? Are these universally optimal?
   - The paper provides no visualization or analysis of what the method actually learns differently. Loss curves, gradient flow analysis, or feature visualizations supporting the noise mitigation hypothesis are absent.
   - No investigation of which layers contribute most to performance gains or whether the benefit concentrates in early, middle, or late layers, which could validate the noise accumulation hypothesis.

## Minor Weaknesses and Questions

1. **Incomplete Baseline Comparisons**: CBS is the only competing model-level curriculum method tested. Other recent curriculum approaches (self-paced learning, teacher-student methods, other adaptive learning rate schemes) should be included for comprehensive evaluation. Additionally, the paper does not compare against other per-layer learning rate adaptation methods from modern optimizers.

2. **Inconsistent Results and Unexplained Anomalies**: 
   - CBS sometimes outperforms LeRaC (e.g., ResNet-18 on CIFAR-100: 72.64% CBS vs. 72.90% LeRaC, within noise). The paper does not characterize when each method is preferable or discuss failure modes.
   - Pre-trained CvT-13 with CBS achieves 85.85% on CIFAR-100 versus 94.15% for conventional training—a large discrepancy suggesting potential implementation or hyperparameter tuning issues that warrant clarification.

3. **Unaddressed Interactions with Modern Training Techniques**: The paper does not discuss how LeRaC interacts with:
   - Batch normalization and layer normalization, which may already mitigate initialization noise and reduce LeRaC's benefit
   - Dropout and other regularization techniques
   - Modern learning rate schedules (cosine annealing, step decay)
   - Adaptive optimizers (Adam, AdamW) where per-parameter adaptation may conflict with per-layer scheduling

4. **Limited Scope and Scalability Questions**:

reviews.original_text:
Summary:
The paper proposes a kind of model-level curriculum learning strategy, which assigns higher initial learning rates to shallow layers than deep ones and continues increasing all learning rates until they reach the same value during the training process. It is a model- and task-agnostic approach. To verify its effectiveness, the authors apply it to multiple domains and different neural networks.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strengths:

1.The proposed strategy concerning layer-wise learning rate is important.

2.The empirical experiments are sufficient and comprehensive to prove the generality and effectiveness of the strategy.

3.This paper is well written and easy to read.

Weaknesses:

1.The relation between this work and curriculum learning is vague.  Curriculum learning is a training strategy that learns from easy to hard, or more generally, in a certain kind of meaningful order. For example, CBS, which is mentioned in the paper, anneals the standard deviation of Gaussian kernels to pass more high-frequency information. However, the relation between this work and CL is not so clear.

2.The discussed related work is not sufficient. Since this work focuses on the layer-wise learning rate, it should be discussed more clearly about how this work is different from the works about layer-wise(or adaptive) learning rate[1][2][5], which is a common and widely adopted strategy. Additionally, training with increasing learning rate in the early stages is a kind of warmup[3][4], the relations between this work and warmup strategies are required to discussed.  The vague position of this work makes its contribution vague.

3.This paper lacks theoretical analysis and does not explain why increasing the learning rates until reaching the same value is reasonable. It is not convincing only with the empirical results. The designed mechanism is neither intuitive nor well theoretically supported, making its justification questionable. The only way for justification is through experiments, which may be tricky, because the proposed method needs more hyper-parameters than that of the conventional methods.

[1] Singh, B., De, S., Zhang, Y., Goldstein, T., & Taylor, G. (2015, December). Layer-specific adaptive learning rates for deep networks. In 2015 IEEE 14th International Conference on Machine Learning and Applications (ICMLA) (pp. 364-368). IEEE.

[2] Ginsburg, B., Gitman, I., & You, Y. (2018). Large batch training of convolutional networks with layer-wise adaptive rate scaling.

[3] Gotmare, A., Keskar, N. S., Xiong, C., & Socher, R. (2018). A closer look at deep learning heuristics: Learning rate restarts, warmup and distillation. 

[4] You, Y., Gitman, I., & Ginsburg, B. (2017). Large batch training of convolutional networks. 

[5] Adam:A Method for Stochastic Optimization. ICLR 2015

Questions:
1. There are many hyperparameters of this strategy, so I’d like to know how to choose proper initial learning rates for all layers and how does the sensitivity of hyperparameters affect the experimental results?
2. For pre-trained models, this paper states that “The same issue can occur if the weights are pre-trained on a distinct task, where the misalignment of the weights with a new task is likely higher for the high-level feature layers. ” However, it is generally believed that the learning rates of the layers close to the outputs are relatively high and the learning rates of the layers close to the inputs are low or even equals to zero in the process of fine-tuning, which seems to be the opposite of the strategy proposed in this paper. How to explain the difference between them?
3. It may be easier to understand Eq. (8), if the authors can plot a figure for μ(j).
4. Whether assigning higher learning rate to the deeper layers can also obtain better performance through hyper-parameter tuning ? The learning landscape of the models is not clear.

Limitations:
The authors do not discuss the limitation. However, there is a lack of both an intuitive explanation or theoretical analysis for the proposed method, making it hard to be a convincing work. A large line of related works is missed, making the contributions vague.

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.

reviews.summary: The manuscript proposes a model-level curriculum learning framework that uses variable learning rates across a DNN to jump-start the learning process. The core idea revolves around compensating for the difference in learning across the depth of a DNN by using larger learning rates closer to the input and gradually decreasing their value towards the output layers. As intended, this curriculum is data-free and removes the need to assess the difficulty of samples, similar to how data-level curriculum operates, and instead can be applied across a range of architectures and tasks. The proposed idea improves on the performance of standard training regimes across vision, language and audio tasks, using residual, recurrent and transformer-based architectures.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths
- The writing offers a clear and understandable representation of the curriculum learning approach.  More importantly, the categorization of various levels of curriculum learning provides important context to the ideas being discussed.
- The depth of experimental evaluation, stretching across datasets, tasks and architectures is commendable.

Weaknesses
- Data augmentation has proven valuable to the training process, be it commonly used approaches like random cropping/flipping or more intricate mixed approaches. They offer substantial improvement in performance and as well as alternative properties of a DNN. The proposed method is not evaluated alongside any common preprocessing techniques (vision datasets), thus offering a gap in the exploration of the behavior of the proposed algorithm alongside them.
- The set of results posted across various tables in the manuscript is commendable. However, in certain cases the the average performance and standard deviation across multiple methods are extremely close. While this does not take away from the final conclusions, the use of significance tests/metrics could help further distinguish improvements.
- The descriptions of the preprocessing and training regimes offer extremely important context to the results presented throughout the paper.
However, an exploration of the two key parameters that make up the proposed method, various update setting for the learning rate and the choice of iteration/epoch at which to converge to the standard learning rate would offer more insight into the proposed method.
reviews.questions:
- Specific to the vision datasets and results, could the authors provide insights into (a) how the use of common preprocessing techniques affects the behavior of the proposed approach, and (b) the balance in peak performance achievable in standard training vs. the proposed method when preprocessing is included. While preprocessing techniques add some element of stochasticity, it is important to understand an algorithm in that context.
- The addition of significance tests/metrics would be beneficial to further emphasize the benefits of the proposed algorithm as well as other areas for potential improvement. Could the authors reassess their results using significance metrics?
- Moving a small portion of the experimental setup to the appendices/supplementary material and including a study of how k (iterations/epochs), from small to absurdly large, affects the improvement in performance offered by the proposed algorithm would add another dimension to the evaluations presented in this work. The results themselves should be a by-product of the suite of experiments already executed so hopefully there is minimal overhead.
- Given the variable levels of improvement, even within the scope of the same task, could the authors discuss plausible schemes that take into account other factors (like data, gradient flow, etc.) that could provide more consistent gains?
- At a more abstract level could the authors address the potential negative implications/limitations of their approach.
reviews.limitations:
While there aren't glaring limitations or issues with the proposed method, the authors can consider potential negative effects in alternative properties of the DNN, as a by-product.
Since there isn't an evaluation of the calibration quality or adversarial robustness of the final solutions, these could be potential issues or limitations to the proposed algorithm. 
Keeping robust/adversarial training or alternative objective functions to classification in mind could lead to unexpected outcomes.

Summary:
The manuscript proposes a model-level curriculum learning framework that uses variable learning rates across a DNN to jump-start the learning process. The core idea revolves around compensating for the difference in learning across the depth of a DNN by using larger learning rates closer to the input and gradually decreasing their value towards the output layers. As intended, this curriculum is data-free and removes the need to assess the difficulty of samples, similar to how data-level curriculum operates, and instead can be applied across a range of architectures and tasks. The proposed idea improves on the performance of standard training regimes across vision, language and audio tasks, using residual, recurrent and transformer-based architectures.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strengths
- The writing offers a clear and understandable representation of the curriculum learning approach.  More importantly, the categorization of various levels of curriculum learning provides important context to the ideas being discussed.
- The depth of experimental evaluation, stretching across datasets, tasks and architectures is commendable.

Weaknesses
- Data augmentation has proven valuable to the training process, be it commonly used approaches like random cropping/flipping or more intricate mixed approaches. They offer substantial improvement in performance and as well as alternative properties of a DNN. The proposed method is not evaluated alongside any common preprocessing techniques (vision datasets), thus offering a gap in the exploration of the behavior of the proposed algorithm alongside them.
- The set of results posted across various tables in the manuscript is commendable. However, in certain cases the the average performance and standard deviation across multiple methods are extremely close. While this does not take away from the final conclusions, the use of significance tests/metrics could help further distinguish improvements.
- The descriptions of the preprocessing and training regimes offer extremely important context to the results presented throughout the paper.
However, an exploration of the two key parameters that make up the proposed method, various update setting for the learning rate and the choice of iteration/epoch at which to converge to the standard learning rate would offer more insight into the proposed method.

Questions:
- Specific to the vision datasets and results, could the authors provide insights into (a) how the use of common preprocessing techniques affects the behavior of the proposed approach, and (b) the balance in peak performance achievable in standard training vs. the proposed method when preprocessing is included. While preprocessing techniques add some element of stochasticity, it is important to understand an algorithm in that context.
- The addition of significance tests/metrics would be beneficial to further emphasize the benefits of the proposed algorithm as well as other areas for potential improvement. Could the authors reassess their results using significance metrics?
- Moving a small portion of the experimental setup to the appendices/supplementary material and including a study of how k (iterations/epochs), from small to absurdly large, affects the improvement in performance offered by the proposed algorithm would add another dimension to the evaluations presented in this work. The results themselves should be a by-product of the suite of experiments already executed so hopefully there is minimal overhead.
- Given the variable levels of improvement, even within the scope of the same task, could the authors discuss plausible schemes that take into account other factors (like data, gradient flow, etc.) that could provide more consistent gains?
- At a more abstract level could the authors address the potential negative implications/limitations of their approach.

Limitations:
While there aren't glaring limitations or issues with the proposed method, the authors can consider potential negative effects in alternative properties of the DNN, as a by-product.
Since there isn't an evaluation of the calibration quality or adversarial robustness of the final solutions, these could be potential issues or limitations to the proposed algorithm. 
Keeping robust/adversarial training or alternative objective functions to classification in mind could lead to unexpected outcomes.

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.text:
# Hybrid Peer Review

## Summary

This manuscript proposes LeRaC (Learning Rate Curriculum), a model-level curriculum learning framework that assigns different learning rates to neural network layers during early training epochs. The core strategy assigns higher learning rates to layers closer to the input and gradually decreases them toward output layers until convergence to a uniform learning rate after k epochs (typically 2-7). The authors motivate this approach by conjecturing that noise from random weight initialization propagates and accumulates through network depth, and argue that their data-free curriculum mitigates this effect without requiring explicit sample difficulty estimation. The method is evaluated across eight datasets spanning vision (CIFAR-10/100, Tiny ImageNet), language (BoolQ, QNLI, RTE), and audio (ESC-50, CREMA-D) domains using diverse architectures including ResNets, DenseNet, BERT, transformers, and LSTMs. Results demonstrate consistent improvements over standard training and generally superior performance compared to Curriculum by Smoothing (CBS).

## Strengths

The paper presents several commendable qualities. First, the **clarity of presentation** is excellent—the writing effectively communicates the approach, and the categorization of curriculum learning levels (data-level, model-level, task-level, objective-level) provides important context that grounds the contribution. 

Second, the **simplicity and generality** of the approach is notable. LeRaC requires no domain-specific modifications or explicit sample difficulty metrics, making it broadly applicable across CNNs, RNNs, and Transformers without architectural changes. This universality across vision, language, and audio domains is a genuine strength.

Third, the **depth of experimental evaluation** is commendable. The breadth of datasets (8 total), architectures (7 distinct types), and domains demonstrates serious empirical effort. Importantly, the **consistency** of improvements across all tested configurations is valuable—unlike CBS, which shows inconsistent results, LeRaC improves performance on all 12 image classification and all 6 text classification tasks, suggesting robustness across domains.

Finally, the **computational efficiency** is significant. Unlike CBS, which adds Gaussian kernel layers and computational overhead, LeRaC introduces no additional training time cost, making it practically deployable.

## Major Weaknesses

Despite these strengths, the paper has several substantial limitations that prevent a stronger recommendation.

### Weak Theoretical Justification

The foundational claim that "noise grows monotonically through layers" (lines 171-176) is stated as a conjecture without rigorous theoretical support. The paper asserts this intuition but provides neither formal analysis nor empirical validation. Critically, the authors never demonstrate that noise actually accumulates in the manner claimed, nor do they provide evidence that their specific learning rate schedule uniquely addresses this problem. While the intuition is reasonable, the connection between assigning lower learning rates to deeper layers and mitigating initialization noise remains unclear. Why should this particular strategy—rather than alternative per-layer adaptation schemes—be optimal? The paper does not explain the mechanistic link between their approach and the proposed noise mitigation hypothesis.

This theoretical gap is compounded by the **limited novelty** of the core technique. Using different learning rates per layer is not fundamentally new; layer-wise learning rate adaptation appears in prior work and modern optimizers. The primary contribution is reframing this as curriculum learning, which feels incremental. Moreover, the connection to curriculum learning itself is somewhat tenuous—the method does not explicitly order samples by difficulty or gradually increase task complexity in a traditional sense. The paper could more accurately characterize this as adaptive regularization rather than classical curriculum learning.

### Modest and Potentially Insignificant Improvements

While consistent, many reported improvements are small. For example, on CIFAR-10 with ResNet-18, the improvement is 0.36% (89.20±0.43 → 89.56±0.16). Similar marginal gains appear throughout the results (often <1%). Critically, **the paper provides no statistical significance testing** despite reporting standard deviations that sometimes overlap with baseline ranges. Given the reported uncertainty bounds, it is unclear whether many improvements are statistically significant or simply within noise margins. For instance, when standard deviations overlap substantially between methods, claims of improvement become questionable. This is a significant methodological gap—the authors should conduct statistical significance tests (e.g., t-tests) to distinguish genuine improvements from noise.

Additionally, there are cases where the comparison is unfavorable or within noise. For example, on CIFAR-100 with ResNet-18, CBS achieves 72.64% while LeRaC achieves 72.90%—a marginal difference that could easily be within measurement error. The paper does not adequately discuss failure modes or cases where LeRaC underperforms.

### Hyperparameter Sensitivity and Tuning Burden

Table 1 reveals a critical tension with the paper's claims of simplicity and universality. The method introduces multiple hyperparameters (k, η₁⁽⁰⁾, ηₙ⁽⁰⁾, c) that require substantial per-architecture tuning. The optimal k ranges from 2-7 epochs, initial learning rates span 5-6 orders of magnitude, and the exponential base is universally set to c=10, yet the paper provides only vague guidance ("5-6 orders of magnitude"). Different architectures require different optimizers, batch sizes, and learning rate ranges, suggesting the method is not as parameter-free or universal as implied.

Critically, the paper does not provide **principled guidance for practitioners** on how to select these hyperparameters for new architectures or datasets without extensive validation. This undermines the "data-free" claim—while the method doesn't require labeled difficulty metrics, it does require careful hyperparameter tuning, which implicitly requires access to validation data or cross-validation. How should practitioners determine k for their specific setup? Is there a principled relationship between architecture depth and optimal k? These questions remain unanswered.

### Insufficient Ablation Studies and Analysis

While Table 5 compares linear versus exponential scheduling, the analysis is limited to a single architecture. The paper does not systematically investigate:
- Sensitivity to the exponential base c=10. Why is this value universally optimal?
- How the initial learning rate ratio (η₁⁽⁰⁾/ηₙ⁽⁰⁾) affects performance across architectures
- Which layers contribute most to performance gains, or whether the benefit concentrates in early/middle/late layers
- Why exponential scheduling is superior to other scheduling functions

Without these ablations, it is unclear whether the method's success depends critically on these design choices or whether simpler alternatives (e.g., linear scheduling) would suffice.

### Missing Mechanistic Understanding and Analysis

The paper lacks **visualizations or empirical analysis** supporting the noise mitigation hypothesis. No loss curves, gradient flow analysis, feature visualizations, or activation statistics are provided to validate the claim that noise grows through layers or that LeRaC specifically addresses this. For instance, measuring gradient norms or activation statistics at different depths with and without LeRaC could provide evidence for the hypothesis. Similarly, analyzing how LeRaC training differs from standard training (e.g., through loss landscape visualization or feature learning dynamics) would strengthen claims about what the method learns differently.

Additionally, the paper does not investigate **interaction with modern training techniques**. How does LeRaC interact with batch normalization, layer normalization, dropout, or adaptive optimizers (Adam, AdamW)? Batch normalization itself addresses initialization effects; does LeRaC provide benefits beyond what normalization already provides? Do per-layer learning rates conflict with adaptive learning rate schemes in modern optimizers? These interactions are not explored.

## Minor Weaknesses

### Incomplete Baseline Comparisons

The paper compares against CBS and standard training but omits comparisons with other relevant methods. No comparison is provided with:
- Other per-layer adaptive learning rate methods (LARS, LAMB, layer-wise learning rate adaptation in modern optimizers)
- Other recent model-level curriculum approaches beyond CBS
- Alternative adaptive optimization schemes

This limits the ability to assess LeRaC's contribution relative to the broader landscape of adaptive training techniques.

### Evaluation Gaps in Vision Domain

The main review correctly notes that the paper does not evaluate LeRaC alongside common preprocessing techniques (random cropping, flipping, mixup, etc.) on vision datasets. This is a significant gap—data augmentation substantially affects training dynamics and final performance. Understanding how LeRaC interacts with these standard techniques is essential for practitioners. Does LeRaC provide additional benefits when augmentation is used? Are improvements consistent across different augmentation strategies?

### Limited Scope and Scalability Questions

All experiments use relatively small models and datasets. It is unclear whether the method scales to:
- Very deep networks (ResNet-152, ViT-Large, billion-parameter models)
- Modern large language models where the noise hypothesis may differ
- Non-classification tasks (regression, object detection, generation tasks)

The claim of universality would be stronger with evidence of scalability to larger, more realistic
